topics.title: Plackett-Burman Design Practice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL508 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Plackett-Burman Design Practice

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plackett–Burman Design (PBD)** is a highly efficient two-level **screening** fractional factorial design developed by Robin L. Plackett and J. P. Burman (1946) for identifying the most significant variables from a large pool of candidate factors using a **minimum number of experimental runs**.

The design is a **Resolution-III** orthogonal design where:
- The total number of runs **N** is always a **multiple of 4** (i.e., N = 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, …).
- It can screen **k = N − 1** factors in **N** runs, with the extra column reserved as a **dummy variable** (used purely for error estimation).
- Every factor is examined at **two coded levels**: a low level **−1** and a high level **+1**.
- The design matrix is generated by **cyclic rotation** of a single generator row.

> [!NOTE]
> **Resolution III** means that **main effects are aliased (confounded) with two-factor interactions**. Hence, PBD is *strictly* a screening tool — *not* an optimization tool. Any significant factor must be taken forward to **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** designs such as **Central Composite Design (CCD)** or **Box–Behnken Design (BBD)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Triage Doctor"

Imagine a hospital emergency ward flooded with **50 possible patient complaints** (factors). A triage doctor (the PBD) cannot treat every case in depth — there is only enough time/beds (runs) for a quick check on each. He assigns a quick **Yes/No** verdict (high level / low level response) to each complaint, identifies the **few critical** cases needing full diagnosis, and sends the rest home. Later, the specialist (RSM/CCD/BBD) performs a deep, quantitative treatment on only those critical few.

| KTU Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| 50 candidate factors | 50 ER patients |
| 12-run PBD | 12 triage slots |
| High/Low coded levels | Severe/Mild symptom check |
| Main effect | "How much does this patient need me?" |
| Significant factors | Critical patients to be admitted |
| Follow-up CCD/BBD | Specialist treatment |

### 1.3 Why PBD is Critical in Bioprocess Engineering

In a typical fermentation/media optimization problem, a researcher may have **10–20 candidate variables**: carbon source, nitrogen source, minerals, vitamins, pH, temperature, agitation, aeration, inoculum age, inoculum size, induction time, etc. Running a full factorial of $2^{20}$ is **impossible** (over a million runs). PBD condenses this to just **12–24 runs**, making it the *industry-standard* first step in any optimization campaign.

### 1.4 Common PBD Variants in KTU Syllabus

| PBD Type | Runs (N) | Factors (k = N−1) | Dummy Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| PBD-8 | 8 | 7 | 0 (use center points / replicates) |
| PBD-12 | 12 | 11 | 1 |
| PBD-16 | 16 | 15 | 1 |
| PBD-20 | 20 | 19 | 1 |
| PBD-24 | 24 | 23 | 1 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If the question says "screen 7 factors" the smallest valid PBD is **PBD-8 (8 runs)**. If it says "screen 11 factors" the answer is **PBD-12 (12 runs)**. Always cross-check: k = N − 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction Logic of the Design Matrix

The Plackett–Burman matrix is built from a **generator row** containing one fewer than N entries, where the **last entry is the negative of the product of all previous entries** (this guarantees orthogonality). The subsequent rows are obtained by **cyclic right-shift** of the previous row.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Cyclic Shifts:** For an N-run PBD, write the first row with (N − 1) entries. Each next row is obtained by shifting the previous row one position to the right; the rightmost element moves to the leftmost position.

### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of Effects

Once the responses (Y) are recorded, the **main effect** of any factor *i* is calculated as:

$$
E_i = \frac{\sum Y_{(+1,i)} - \sum Y_{(-1,i)}}{N/2}
$$

where:
- $\sum Y_{(+1,i)}$ = sum of responses for all runs where factor *i* is at the **+1** level.
- $\sum Y_{(-1,i)}$ = sum of responses for all runs where factor *i* is at the **−1** level.
- $N/2$ = number of runs at each level (for balanced designs).

Equivalently (using means):

$$
E_i = 2 \left( \bar{Y}_{(+1,i)} - \bar{Y}_{(-1,i)} \right)
$$

### 2.3 Error Estimation (Three Methods)

> [!NOTE]
> Since PBD is a **Resolution-III** design, **no internal degrees of freedom** exist for pure error in the standard form. KTU expects you to know **three** ways to obtain an error estimate:

| Method | Formula | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Dummy Variable(s)** | MSE = SS(dummy) / df(dummy) | When N = 12, 20, 24, 28… (≥ 1 dummy column) |
| **Center Points** | MSE = Σ(Y_c − Ȳ_c)² / (n_c − 1) | Always possible; minimum 3 recommended |
| **Replicate Runs** | MSE = Σ(Y_r − Ȳ_r)² / (n_r − 1) | When additional runs are feasible |

### 2.4 Standard Error of an Effect

$$
SE(E_i) = \frac{2 \, \sigma}{\sqrt{N}} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{MSE}{N}}
$$

where $\sigma$ is the true (or estimated) standard deviation of the response, and MSE is the mean squared error from the chosen error-estimation method.

### 2.5 t-Test for Significance

$$
t_i = \frac{E_i}{SE(E_i)} = \frac{E_i \sqrt{N}}{2 \sqrt{MSE}}
$$

Compare $|t_i|$ with the **critical t-value** $t_{(\alpha/2,\, df_{error})}$ from the Student's t-table.

- If $|t_i| > t_{critical}$ → **Factor i is significant** ✓
- If $|t_i| \leq t_{critical}$ → **Factor i is insignificant** ✗

### 2.6 p-Value Approach (Modern KTU preference)

$$
p_i = 2 \times P(T_{df} \geq \vert t_i \vert)
$$

Reject null hypothesis $H_0$ (effect = 0) if $p_i < \alpha$ (typically $\alpha = 0.05$).

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of factors | $k = N - 1$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Main effect | $E_i = \frac{\sum Y_{(+1)} - \sum Y_{(-1)}}{N/2}$ | Same units as response |
| 3 | Mean squared error (center points) | $MSE = \frac{\sum (Y_c - \bar{Y}_c)^2}{n_c - 1}$ | Variance units |
| 4 | Standard error of effect | $SE(E_i) = 2 \sqrt{MSE / N}$ | Same units as $E_i$ |
| 5 | t-statistic | $t_i = E_i / SE(E_i)$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Confidence interval (95%) | $E_i \pm t_{0.025, df} \cdot SE(E_i)$ | Range estimate |
| 7 | Variance of effect | $Var(E_i) = 4 \sigma^2 / N$ | Variance units |
| 8 | Coded level conversion | $X_{coded} = \frac{X_{actual} - (X_{high} + X_{low})/2}{(X_{high} - X_{low})/2}$ | ±1 scaling |
| 9 | Sum of squares (factor) | $SS_i = \frac{N}{4} E_i^2$ | Squared response units |
| 10 | Percent contribution | $PC_i (\%) = \frac{SS_i}{\sum SS_i} \times 100$ | % of total variation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse** $SE(E_i)$ (standard error of an effect) with the standard deviation of a *single* response. The factor of **2** in the formula comes from the difference-of-means structure; this is a frequent KTU trap.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

PBD is the **statistical backbone** of:
- **Media optimization** in industrial fermentation (penicillin, ethanol, citric acid).
- **Critical variable identification** in enzyme production (amylase, protease, lipase).
- **Upstream process development** in biopharmaceuticals (monoclonal antibody titre optimization in CHO cells).
- **Bioremediation studies** to find which environmental factors most influence microbial degradation kinetics.
- **Food & brewery industries** for screening yeast nutrients and process conditions.

The result of a PBD — i.e., the 2–4 significant factors — feeds directly into a **CCD or BBD** for building a quadratic response surface and locating the true optimum.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Case Study: PBD-8 for Screening 7 Factors Affecting Biomass Production

**Background:** A B.Tech project student is producing *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* biomass in shake flasks and has 7 candidate variables to screen.

| Code | Factor | Low Level (−1) | High Level (+1) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Glucose (g/L) | 10 | 30 |
| B | Yeast extract (g/L) | 2 | 6 |
| C | KH₂PO₄ (g/L) | 0.5 | 2.0 |
| D | MgSO₄·7H₂O (g/L) | 0.2 | 1.0 |
| E | Temperature (°C) | 28 | 34 |
| F | pH | 4.5 | 6.0 |
| G | Agitation (rpm) | 120 | 200 |

### 3.2 The PBD-8 Design Matrix (Cyclic Construction)

The **generator row** is chosen as: `(+ + − − + + −)` for factors A B C D E F G.
Each next row is the **cyclic right-shift** of the previous row.

| Run | A | B | C | D | E | F | G | Response Y (g/L biomass) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---:|
| 1 | **+** | **+** | − | − | **+** | **+** | − | 2.5 |
| 2 | − | **+** | **+** | − | − | **+** | **+** | 3.8 |
| 3 | **+** | − | **+** | **+** | − | − | **+** | 2.1 |
| 4 | **+** | **+** | − | **+** | **+** | − | − | 4.2 |
| 5 | − | **+** | **+** | − | **+** | **+** | − | 3.5 |
| 6 | − | − | **+** | **+** | − | **+** | **+** | 2.8 |
| 7 | **+** | − | − | − | **+** | − | **+** | 4.5 |
| 8 | − | − | − | − | − | − | − | 1.8 |

**Verification of Orthogonality (sum of each column should be 0):**
- A: +1 −1 +1 +1 −1 −1 +1 −1 = **0** ✓
- B: +1 +1 −1 +1 +1 −1 −1 −1 = **0** ✓
- C: −1 +1 +1 −1 +1 +1 −1 −1 = **0** ✓
- D: −1 −1 +1 +1 −1 +1 −1 −1 = **0** ✓
- E: +1 −1 −1 +1 +1 −1 +1 −1 = **0** ✓
- F: +1 +1 −1 −1 +1 +1 −1 −1 = **0** ✓
- G: −1 +1 +1 −1 −1 +1 +1 −1 = **0** ✓

### 3.3 Calculation of Main Effects (Factor A — Glucose)

**Identify runs at +1 level for A:** Runs 1, 3, 4, 7 → Responses: 2.5, 2.1, 4.2, 4.5
**Identify runs at −1 level for A:** Runs 2, 5, 6, 8 → Responses: 3.8, 3.5, 2.8, 1.8

$$
\sum Y_{(+1,A)} = 2.5 + 2.1 + 4.2 + 4.5 = 13.3
$$

$$
\sum Y_{(-1,A)} = 3.8 + 3.5 + 2.8 + 1.8 = 11.9
$$

$$
E_A = \frac{13.3 - 11.9}{8/2} = \frac{1.4}{4} = +0.35
$$

**Interpretation:** Increasing glucose from 10 to 30 g/L **increases** biomass by 0.35 g/L on average.

### 3.4 Calculation of Main Effect (Factor B — Yeast Extract)

**Runs at +1 for B:** Runs 1, 2, 4, 5 → Responses: 2.5, 3.8, 4.2, 3.5
**Runs at −1 for B:** Runs 3, 6, 7, 8 → Responses: 2.1, 2.8, 4.5, 1.8

$$
\sum Y_{(+1,B)} = 2.5 + 3.8 + 4.2 + 3.5 = 14.0
$$

$$
\sum Y_{(-1,B)} = 2.1 + 2.8 + 4.5 + 1.8 = 11.2
$$

$$
E_B = \frac{14.0 - 11.2}{4} = \frac{2.8}{4} = +0.70
$$

### 3.5 Calculation of Main Effect (Factor C — KH₂PO₄)

**Runs at +1 for C:** Runs 2, 3, 5, 6 → Responses: 3.8, 2.1, 3.5, 2.8
**Runs at −1 for C:** Runs 1, 4, 7, 8 → Responses: 2.5, 4.2, 4.5, 1.8

$$
\sum Y_{(+1,C)} = 3.8 + 2.1 + 3.5 + 2.8 = 12.2
$$

$$
\sum Y_{(-1,C)} = 2.5 + 4.2 + 4.5 + 1.8 = 13.0
$$

$$
E_C = \frac{12.2 - 13.0}{4} = \frac{-0.8}{4} = -0.20
$$

### 3.6 Calculation of Main Effect (Factor D — MgSO₄)

**Runs at +1 for D:** Runs 3, 4, 6, 7 → Responses: 2.1, 4.2, 2.8, 4.5
**Runs at −1 for D:** Runs 1, 2, 5, 8 → Responses: 2.5, 3.8, 3.5, 1.8

$$
\sum Y_{(+1,D)} = 2.1 + 4.2 + 2.8 + 4.5 = 13.6
$$

$$
\sum Y_{(-1,D)} = 2.5 + 3.8 + 3.5 + 1.8 = 11.6
$$

$$
E_D = \frac{13.6 - 11.6}{4} = \frac{2.0}{4} = +0.50
$$

### 3.7 Calculation of Main Effect (Factor E — Temperature)

**Runs at +1 for E:** Runs 1, 4, 5, 7 → Responses: 2.5, 4.2, 3.5, 4.5
**Runs at −1 for E:** Runs 2, 3, 6, 8 → Responses: 3.8, 2.1, 2.8, 1.8

$$
\sum Y_{(+1,E)} = 2.5 + 4.2 + 3.5 + 4.5 = 14.7
$$

$$
\sum Y_{(-1,E)} = 3.8 + 2.1 + 2.8 + 1.8 = 10.5
$$

$$
E_E = \frac{14.7 - 10.5}{4} = \frac{4.2}{4} = +1.05
$$

### 3.8 Calculation of Main Effect (Factor F — pH)

**Runs at +1 for F:** Runs 1, 2, 5, 6 → Responses: 2.5, 3.8, 3.5, 2.8
**Runs at −1 for F:** Runs 3, 4, 7, 8 → Responses: 2.1, 4.2, 4.5, 1.8

$$
\sum Y_{(+1,F)} = 2.5 + 3.8 + 3.5 + 2.8 = 12.6
$$

$$
\sum Y_{(-1,F)} = 2.1 + 4.2 + 4.5 + 1.8 = 12.6
$$

$$
E_F = \frac{12.6 - 12.6}{4} = 0.00
$$

### 3.9 Calculation of Main Effect (Factor G — Agitation)

**Runs at +1 for G:** Runs 2, 3, 6, 7 → Responses: 3.8, 2.1, 2.8, 4.5
**Runs at −1 for G:** Runs 1, 4, 5, 8 → Responses: 2.5, 4.2, 3.5, 1.8

$$
\sum Y_{(+1,G)} = 3.8 + 2.1 + 2.8 + 4.5 = 13.2
$$

$$
\sum Y_{(-1,G)} = 2.5 + 4.2 + 3.5 + 1.8 = 12.0
$$

$$
E_G = \frac{13.2 - 12.0}{4} = \frac{1.2}{4} = +0.30
$$

### 3.10 Master Effect Summary Table

| Factor | $\sum Y_{(+1)}$ | $\sum Y_{(-1)}$ | Effect $E_i$ | Rank by |E| |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A — Glucose | 13.3 | 11.9 | +0.35 | 3 |
| B — Yeast extract | 14.0 | 11.2 | +0.70 | 2 |
| C — KH₂PO₄ | 12.2 | 13.0 | −0.20 | 6 |
| D — MgSO₄ | 13.6 | 11.6 | +0.50 | 4 |
| E — Temperature | 14.7 | 10.5 | **+1.05** | 1 |
| F — pH | 12.6 | 12.6 | 0.00 | 7 |
| G — Agitation | 13.2 | 12.0 | +0.30 | 5 |

### 3.11 Error Estimation Using 3 Center Points

Three additional center-point runs (at mid-level of all factors) gave:
- $Y_{c1} = 3.2$ g/L, $Y_{c2} = 3.0$ g/L, $Y_{c3} = 3.1$ g/L

**Step 1 — Mean of center points:**

$$
\bar{Y}_c = \frac{3.2 + 3.0 + 3.1}{3} = 3.10
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of squared deviations:**

$$
SS_{center} = (3.2 - 3.1)^2 + (3.0 - 3.1)^2 + (3.1 - 3.1)^2
$$

$$
SS_{center} = (0.1)^2 + (-0.1)^2 + (0)^2 = 0.01 + 0.01 + 0 = 0.02
$$

**Step 3 — Mean squared error (df = n_c − 1 = 2):**

$$
MSE = \frac{0.02}{3 - 1} = \frac{0.02}{2} = 0.01
$$

**Step 4 — Standard error of each effect:**

$$
SE(E_i) = 2 \sqrt{\frac{MSE}{N}} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{0.01}{8}} = 2 \times 0.0354 = 0.0707
$$

### 3.12 t-Test and Significance Decision

**Critical value:** $t_{(0.025, \, df=2)} = 4.303$ at $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed).

$$
t_i = \frac{E_i}{SE(E_i)} = \frac{E_i}{0.0707}
$$

| Factor | $E_i$ | $t_i = E_i / 0.0707$ | $\vert t_i \vert$ | $\vert t_i \vert > 4.303$? | Significant? |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A — Glucose | +0.35 | +4.95 | 4.95 | ✓ | **YES** |
| B — Yeast extract | +0.70 | +9.90 | 9.90 | ✓ | **YES** |
| C — KH₂PO₄ | −0.20 | −2.83 | 2.83 | ✗ | No |
| D — MgSO₄ | +0.50 | +7.07 | 7.07 | ✓ | **YES** |
| E — Temperature | +1.05 | +14.85 | 14.85 | ✓ | **YES** |
| F — pH | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | ✗ | No |
| G — Agitation | +0.30 | +4.24 | 4.24 | ✗ (marginal) | No |

### 3.13 Engineering Conclusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significant factors:** A (Glucose), B (Yeast extract), D (MgSO₄), E (Temperature).
> **Insignificant factors:** C (KH₂PO₄), F (pH), G (Agitation) — held at mid-level in the next stage.
> **Next experimental design:** A **Central Composite Design (CCD)** or **Box–Behnken Design (BBD)** with the 4 significant factors (A, B, D, E) to locate the *true optimum* of biomass production.

### 3.14 Percent Contribution of Each Factor

For ranking and communication, KTU expects the **percent contribution** to total sum of squares:

$$
SS_i = \frac{N}{4} E_i^2 = \frac{8}{4} E_i^2 = 2 E_i^2
$$

| Factor | $E_i$ | $SS_i = 2 E_i^2$ | PC (%) |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| E — Temperature | +1.05 | 2.2050 | **42.4** |
| B — Yeast extract | +0.70 | 0.9800 | **18.8** |
| D — MgSO₄ | +0.50 | 0.5000 | 9.6 |
| A — Glucose | +0.35 | 0.2450 | 4.7 |
| G — Agitation | +0.30 | 0.1800 | 3.5 |
| C — KH₂PO₄ | −0.20 | 0.0800 | 1.5 |
| F — pH | 0.00 | 0.0000 | 0.0 |
| **Error (from center points)** | — | **1.0120** | **19.5** |
| **Total** | — | **5.2020** | **100.0** |

> [!TIP]
> **Temperature alone contributes 42.4%** of the total variability — a typical "star variable" in microbial bioprocesses. This justifies prioritizing it for tight control in industrial scale-up.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — PBD Experimental Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[STEP 1: PROBLEM DEFINITION\nDefine objective and response variable Y]
    nodeB[STEP 2: FACTOR IDENTIFICATION\nList all candidate variables from literature]
    nodeC[STEP 3: LEVEL SELECTION\nChoose low -1 and high +1 levels for each factor]
    nodeD[STEP 4: DESIGN CONSTRUCTION\nBuild N x k PBD matrix using cyclic shifts]
    nodeE[STEP 5: RANDOMIZATION\nRandomize run order to remove bias]
    nodeF[STEP 6: EXPERIMENTAL EXECUTION\nConduct N factorial runs and n_c center points]
    nodeG[STEP 7: DATA RECORDING\nTabulate Y values against design matrix]
    nodeH[STEP 8: EFFECT CALCULATION\nCompute main effect E_i for each factor]
    nodeI[STEP 9: ERROR ESTIMATION\nCompute MSE from center points or dummy variables]
    nodeJ[STEP 10: STATISTICAL TESTING\nApply t-test: compare vert t_i vert with t critical]
    nodeK[STEP 11: SIGNIFICANT FACTORS\nIdentify the 2 to 4 critical variables]
    nodeL[STEP 12: NEXT STAGE RSM\nProceed to CCD or BBD with significant factors]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL

    style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeL fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeJ fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeK fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Selecting the Correct PBD Size

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[START: How many candidate factors k]
    q1{Is k a multiple of 4 minus 1?}
    n4[Use PBD-8 for k = 7]
    n12[Use PBD-12 for k = 11]
    n20[Use PBD-20 for k = 19]
    n24[Use PBD-24 for k = 23]
    round[Round k UP to nearest valid value]
    check{N k greater than 24?}
    spc[Use Fractional Factorial or Taguchi L_array]
    note1[For PBD-8 use center points for error]
    note2[For PBD-12 onward use 1 dummy column for error]

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|k = 7| n4
    q1 -->|k = 11| n12
    q1 -->|k = 19| n20
    q1 -->|k = 23| n24
    q1 -->|No match| round
    round --> check
    check -->|No| q1
    check -->|Yes| spc
    n4 --> note1
    n12 --> note2
    n20 --> note2
    n24 --> note2

    style start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style n4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style n12 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style spc fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — PBD Analysis Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT BLOCK\nDesign Matrix N x k\nand Response Vector Y] --> B[BLOCK 1\nColumn-wise Sum\nat Plus 1 and Minus 1]
    B --> C[BLOCK 2\nEffect Calculation\nE_i = Sum diff divided by N over 2]
    C --> D[BLOCK 3\nError Pool\nCenter points or Dummies]
    D --> E[BLOCK 4\nMSE and SE Computation]
    E --> F[BLOCK 5\nt_statistic Calculation]
    F --> G[BLOCK 6\nComparison with t critical]
    G --> H{DECISION DIAMOND\nvert t_i vert greater than t critical}
    H -->|Yes| I[SIGNIFICANT\nPush to RSM Stage]
    H -->|No| J[INSIGNIFICANT\nHold at center level]

    style A fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Hierarchical Block Diagram — Position of PBD in Bioprocess Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[LEVEL 1: FACTOR IDENTIFICATION\nLiterature review and prior knowledge]
    L2[LEVEL 2: SCREENING\nPlackett Burman Design PBD]
    L3[LEVEL 3: OPTIMIZATION\nCentral Composite Design CCD or Box Behnken BBD]
    L4[LEVEL 4: VERIFICATION\nConfirmation runs at predicted optimum]
    L5[LEVEL 5: SCALE UP\nLab to Pilot to Industrial Bioreactor]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5

    style L1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L5 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice how PBD occupies **Level 2** — it is *not* the final destination. Skipping from Level 1 straight to Level 3 (CCD/BBD) without PBD is a common KTU mistake; CCD/BBD cannot tell you which factors *don't* matter, only refine those that already do.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define Plackett–Burman Design. List any four of its salient features. Mention two situations in bioprocess engineering where it is the design of choice.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** Plackett–Burman Design (PBD) is a two-level fractional factorial screening design developed by Plackett and Burman (1946), used for identifying the most significant factors from a large pool of candidate variables with a **minimum number of experimental runs**.

**Salient Features (any 4):**
1. It is a **Resolution-III** orthogonal design — main effects are unconfounded with each other but confounded with two-factor interactions.
2. The total number of runs **N must be a multiple of 4** (N = 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24 …).
3. It can screen **k = N − 1** factors in just **N** runs.
4. The design matrix is generated by **cyclic rotation** of a single generator row.
5. Every factor is studied at only **two levels** (coded as −1 and +1).
6. Error estimation is done via **dummy variables** (when N ≥ 12) or **center points**.

**Two Bioprocess Situations:**
1. Screening 10–15 medium components (carbon, nitrogen, minerals, vitamins) to identify the 2–4 most influential for a fermentation titre.
2. Identifying which process parameters (pH, temperature, agitation, aeration, inoculum size) most critically affect enzyme / biomass production.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**What is a dummy variable in Plackett–Burman Design? How is it used to estimate experimental error? Show the relevant formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **dummy variable** is an additional column in the PBD matrix (available when N is a multiple of 4 and not all N − 1 columns are assigned to real factors) that is **treated like a real factor during the experiment but represents no physical quantity**. It carries only experimental noise.

**Procedure for Error Estimation:**
1. The response values at the +1 and −1 levels of the dummy column are summed.
2. The "effect" of the dummy $E_d$ is calculated just like any real factor.
3. The **sum of squares** of the dummy is: $SS_d = (N/4) \cdot E_d^2$.
4. With $n_d$ dummy columns, the **mean squared error** is:

$$
MSE = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n_d} SS_{d,j}}{n_d}
$$

5. The standard error of any real effect becomes:

$$
SE(E_i) = 2 \sqrt{\frac{MSE}{N}}
$$

**Significance test:** $t_i = E_i / SE(E_i)$, compared against $t_{(\alpha/2,\, n_d)}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations**, students must answer **one full question (14 marks)** from a choice of two. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Model solutions below are fully worked out for self-study.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — *Numerical / Design Construction Type***

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** Construct a Plackett–Burman design matrix to screen **7 factors (A, B, C, D, E, F, G)** in **8 runs**. State the construction rule and verify the orthogonality of any one column. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following PBD-12 experiment was conducted to screen 11 factors (A–K) with 1 dummy column. The effects of each factor and the dummy are:

$$
E_A=12.5,\; E_B=3.2,\; E_C=8.7,\; E_D=1.5,\; E_E=15.6,\; E_F=2.1,\; E_G=9.8,\; E_H=0.8,\; E_I=4.3,\; E_J=1.9,\; E_K=6.5,\; E_{dummy}=0.5
$$

If $MSE = 0.36$ (from the dummy) and $N = 12$, identify the **significant factors** at $\alpha = 0.05$ using the t-test. ($t_{critical} = t_{0.025, 1} = 12.706$). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction (7 marks):**

The PBD-8 matrix for 7 factors A, B, C, D, E, F, G with 8 runs is constructed using the **generator row**: $(+ + - - + + -)$ for factors A, B, C, D, E, F, G, with subsequent rows being **cyclic right-shifts**.

| Run | A | B | C | D | E | F | G |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | + | + | − | − | + | + | − |
| 2 | − | + | + | − | − | + | + |
| 3 | + | − | + | + | − | − | + |
| 4 | + | + | − | + | + | − | − |
| 5 | − | + | + | − | + | + | − |
| 6 | − | − | + | + | − | + | + |
| 7 | + | − | − | − | + | − | + |
| 8 | − | − | − | − | − | − | − |

**Orthogonality Check for Column A:**
Sum of Column A = $(+1) + (-1) + (+1) + (+1) + (-1) + (-1) + (+1) + (-1) = 0$ ✓

This confirms that the design is **orthogonal**, ensuring that the estimate of each main effect is **independent** of the others.

**Part (b) — Significance Testing (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Standard error of each effect:**

$$
SE(E_i) = 2 \sqrt{\frac{MSE}{N}} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{0.36}{12}} = 2 \times \sqrt{0.03} = 2 \times 0.1732 = 0.3464
$$

**Step 2 — t-statistic for each factor:**

$$
t_i = \frac{E_i}{0.3464}
$$

| Factor | $E_i$ | $t_i$ | $\vert t_i \vert$ | $\vert t_i \vert > 12.706$? | Significant? |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A | 12.5 | 36.08 | 36.08 | ✓ | **YES** |
| B | 3.2 | 9.24 | 9.24 | ✗ | No |
| C | 8.7 | 25.11 | 25.11 | ✓ | **YES** |
| D | 1.5 | 4.33 | 4.33 | ✗ | No |
| E | 15.6 | 45.03 | 45.03 | ✓ | **YES** |
| F | 2.1 | 6.06 | 6.06 | ✗ | No |
| G | 9.8 | 28.29 | 28.29 | ✓ | **YES** |
| H | 0.8 | 2.31 | 2.31 | ✗ | No |
| I | 4.3 | 12.41 | 12.41 | ✗ (marginal) | No |
| J | 1.9 | 5.48 | 5.48 | ✗ | No |
| K | 6.5 | 18.76 | 18.76 | ✓ | **YES** |

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

**Significant factors** (in rank order of $|E_i|$): **E (15.6), A (12.5), G (9.8), C (8.7), K (6.5)**.
**Insignificant factors:** B, D, F, H, I, J — these will be held at their mid-levels in the next RSM stage.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — *Conceptual / Comparative Type***

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** Compare Plackett–Burman Design with **Full Factorial Design** and **One-Factor-at-a-Time (OFAT)** approach. List three advantages and two limitations of PBD. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a PBD-8 experiment for 7 factors, the main effects (in coded units) were found to be:
$E_A = 5.2,\; E_B = 1.1,\; E_C = 7.8,\; E_D = 0.4,\; E_E = 3.6,\; E_F = -2.1,\; E_G = 0.9$
with $MSE = 0.81$ (from 3 center points, $N = 8$). Construct a **Pareto chart of standardized effects** $(t_i = E_i / \sqrt{2 MSE / N})$ and identify the **most influential factor**. Comment on the factor $F$ with a **negative effect**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | OFAT | Full Factorial (2ᵏ) | Plackett–Burman |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of runs | $2k$ | $2^k$ (exponential) | $N = k + 1$ (linear) |
| Interaction detection | None | Yes (all) | No (Resolution III) |
| Factor interaction ignored | Yes | No | Yes (assumed negligible) |
| Suitable # factors | Few (≤4) | Few (≤5) | Many (up to 23) |
| Optimization capability | No | Yes (with RSM) | **No — screening only** |
| Error estimation | Replicates only | Internal d.f. available | Dummy / center points |
| Statistical power | Low | High | Moderate-High for screening |

**Three Advantages of PBD:**
1. **Drastically reduced runs** — screening 11 factors in 12 runs (vs 2048 in $2^{11}$).
2. **Orthogonal** — main effects estimated independently without correlation.
3. **Economical** of time, material, and manpower — ideal for preliminary bioprocess optimization.

**Two Limitations of PBD:**
1. **Main effects are confounded with two-factor interactions** (Resolution III) — cannot separate them.
2. **No optimization** — only identifies the "important" factors; a follow-up RSM (CCD/BBD) is mandatory for finding the optimum.

**Part (b) — Pareto Chart Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute SE of an effect:**

$$
SE(E_i) = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, MSE}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.81}{8}} = \sqrt{0.2025} = 0.45
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the slightly different form here: $SE = \sqrt{2MSE/N}$ is equivalent to $2\sqrt{MSE/N}$ in the form we used earlier; both give the **standard error of the effect itself** (this alternate form is widely used in Minitab / Design-Expert).

**Step 2 — Compute standardized effect (t-ratio) for each factor:**

$$
t_i = \frac{E_i}{0.45}
$$

| Factor | $E_i$ | $t_i = E_i / 0.45$ | $\vert t_i \vert$ |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A | +5.2 | +11.56 | 11.56 |
| B | +1.1 | +2.44 | 2.44 |
| C | +7.8 | **+17.33** | **17.33** |
| D | +0.4 | +0.89 | 0.89 |
| E | +3.6 | +8.00 | 8.00 |
| F | −2.1 | −4.67 | 4.67 |
| G | +0.9 | +2.00 | 2.00 |

**Step 3 — Pareto ranking (largest to smallest $|t_i|$):**

$$
C (17.33) \; > \; A (11.56) \; > \; E (8.00) \; > \; F (4.67) \; > \; B (2.44) \; > \; G (2.00) \; > \; D (0.89)
$$

**Step 4 — Most influential factor:**

> **Factor C** is the most influential, with a standardized effect of **+17.33**. Increasing factor C from its low level to its high level produces the **largest positive shift** in the response.

**Step 5 — Interpretation of Factor F (negative effect):**

> **Factor F has a negative effect** ($E_F = -2.1$). This means that **shifting F from its low level to its high level decreases the response**. Operationally, the investigator should consider either:
> 1. Keeping F at its **low level** in the next optimization stage, **or**
> 2. If higher values of F are physically required, recognize that F will **counteract** the gains made by the positive factors (A, C, E).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of effect matters.** Do not just report the absolute value; always interpret the sign. A *negative* significant effect means the *high* level is *worse* than the *low* level.
> 2. **Critical t-value mismatch:** Many students use $t_{0.05, 2} = 2.92$ (one-tailed) instead of $t_{0.025, 2} = 4.303$ (two-tailed). KTU **always** expects **two-tailed** tests for factor screening.
> 3. **Pareto threshold:** The critical t-value at $\alpha = 0.05$ should be drawn as a **vertical line** on the Pareto chart; bars crossing this line are significant.
> 4. **SE formula confusion:** $SE = 2\sqrt{MSE/N}$ (effect SE) and $SE = \sqrt{MSE/N}$ (mean SE) are commonly interchanged. Mark examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this error.
> 5. **Resolution III caveat:** Do not write "PBD detects interactions" — it **cannot**, by design. This is a classic 1-mark deduction trap.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for KTU viva / written exams:**

- [ ] **PBD = Plackett–Burman Design** — a *screening* (not optimization) tool.
- [ ] **N is a multiple of 4** (4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28 …).
- [ ] **k = N − 1** factors screened in N runs.
- [ ] **Resolution III** — main effects confounded with two-factor interactions.
- [ ] **Two coded levels:** −1 (low) and +1 (high).
- [ ] **Cyclic shift construction** of the design matrix from a single generator row.
- [ ] **Effect formula:** $E_i = \frac{\sum Y_{(+1)} - \sum Y_{(-1)}}{N/2}$ (most important formula).
- [ ] **Error estimation** by (a) dummy variable, (b) center points, or (c) replicates.
- [ ] **MSE** = sum of squared deviations of center points divided by $(n_c - 1)$.
- [ ] **SE(Effect)** = $2\sqrt{MSE/N}$ — do not confuse with mean SE.
- [ ] **t-statistic** = Effect / SE(Effect); compare $|t_i|$ with $t_{(\alpha/2, df)}$.
- [ ] **Sign of effect** matters — positive = high level increases response; negative = high level decreases response.
- [ ] **Pareto chart** ranks standardized effects $|t_i|$ in descending order.
- [ ] **Significant factors → CCD or BBD** for true optimization (Level 2 → Level 3 in the bioprocess optimization hierarchy).
- [ ] **Insignificant factors** are held at center/mid-level in the next stage.
- [ ] **Percent contribution** $PC_i = (SS_i / \sum SS_j) \times 100$, where $SS_i = (N/4) E_i^2$.
- [ ] PBD saves **time, cost, and resources** in early-stage bioprocess development.
- [ ] Industrial applications: **media optimization, enzyme production, fermentation yield improvement, bioremediation kinetics, food & brewery process development**.
- [ ] **Always report** N, k, the design matrix, the response vector, the effects, the SE, the t-values, and the conclusion.
- [ ] **Avoid** the words "PBD optimizes" — it **screens**; only CCD/BBD *optimizes*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microbial Cell Growth Kinetics: Growth of Microorganisms, Estimation of Monod Parameters and Temperature Effect on Growth - Estimation of Energy of Activation and Arrhenius Constant for Microorganisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL508 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Microbial Cell Growth Kinetics: Growth of Microorganisms, Estimation of Monod Parameters and Temperature Effect on Growth - Estimation of Energy of Activation and Arrhenius Constant for Microorganisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Microbial Cell Growth

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
**Microbial cell growth** is the orderly increase in all cellular constituents (proteins, RNA, DNA, lipids, polysaccharides) accompanied by an increase in cell number, cell mass, or cell volume. In bioprocess engineering, microbial growth refers to the increase in **biomass concentration (X, g/L)** with respect to time in a controlled bioreactor environment, typically quantified using the **specific growth rate (μ, h⁻¹)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Growth is *not* merely an increase in size of an individual cell. It is a balanced, coordinated process where the concentration of every intracellular constituent increases at the same rate, resulting in an exponential increase in cell number or biomass.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **single seed** sprouting in a fertile field. One seed becomes two saplings, two become four, four become eight, and so on — each generation producing more cells than the last. Microbial growth works the same way under ideal conditions: a single **E. coli** cell can produce over a billion descendants in just **10 hours** through binary fission. The field must have the right **nutrients (glucose)**, **temperature (~37°C)**, and **pH (~7.0)** — exactly the parameters you control in a fermenter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Questions frequently ask students to differentiate between *growth* (population increase) and *individual cell enlargement*. Only population-level increases under balanced conditions are called "growth."

---

## 1.2 The Microbial Growth Curve

When a microbial population is inoculated into a fresh, sterile nutrient medium in a closed system (batch culture), the **cell density vs. time** profile follows a characteristic sigmoidal pattern with four distinct phases.

### The Four Canonical Phases

| Phase | Defining Characteristic | Key Observation |
|-------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **Lag Phase** | Cells adapt to new environment; no division | Constant cell density, increased cell size |
| **Exponential (Log) Phase** | Cells divide at maximum constant rate | Linear increase of ln(X) vs. time |
| **Stationary Phase** | Growth rate = death rate (nutrient depletion) | Plateau in biomass |
| **Death (Decline) Phase** | Cells die faster than they are produced | Exponential decrease in viable cells |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** "**L**azy **E**ngineers **S**tudy **D**eath" → **L**ag → **E**xponential → **S**tationary → **D**eath.

---

## 1.3 Monod Parameters (The Saturation Model)

### Formal Definition
The **Monod equation** is an empirical, saturation-type kinetic model that relates the **specific growth rate (μ)** of a microbial population to the **limiting substrate concentration (S)**. It is the microbial analogue of the **Michaelis-Menten equation** in enzyme kinetics.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a restaurant with **50 seats**. No matter how many hungry customers arrive, only 50 can be served at once — beyond that, the kitchen is "saturated." Similarly, microbes can only process substrate up to a maximum rate (μmax). Adding more sugar won't make them grow infinitely faster; their internal machinery becomes the bottleneck.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Monod kinetics is a *single-substrate limitation* model. It assumes only ONE nutrient is rate-limiting. For multi-substrate limitations, models like the *Diauxic model* or *Blackman kinetics* are used.

---

## 1.4 Temperature Effect on Growth & Arrhenius Framework

### Formal Definition
**Temperature** is a critical process parameter that influences microbial growth by affecting the kinetics of intracellular enzymatic reactions. The temperature-growth relationship is non-monotonic: below an optimum, growth increases with temperature (Arrhenius regime); above the optimum, enzymes denature and growth rate falls precipitously.

The **Arrhenius equation** is the fundamental model that quantifies the temperature dependence of the specific growth rate (or any rate constant) of a microbial system through two characteristic parameters: the **activation energy (Ea, kJ/mol)** and the **Arrhenius constant (A, h⁻¹)**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **chemical reaction needing a push** to start — like pushing a rock over a hill. The **height of the hill** = activation energy (Ea). The **frequency of attempts** = Arrhenius constant (A). A small rise in temperature gives molecules more kinetic energy, helping them "jump the hill" more often — explaining why microbial rates typically double for every **10°C** rise (the **Q₁₀ rule**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Constants to Memorize:**
> - **Universal Gas Constant (R): 8.314 J/(mol·K) = 8.314 × 10⁻³ kJ/(mol·K)**
> - **Q₁₀ ≈ 2** for most biological systems
> - **Reference Temperature (T_ref): 298.15 K (25°C)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation of Microbial Growth

### 2.1.1 Specific Growth Rate (μ)
The **specific growth rate** is defined as the rate of biomass production per unit biomass present:

$$\mu = \frac{1}{X} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt}$$

where:
- $\mu$ = specific growth rate (**h⁻¹**)
- $X$ = biomass concentration (**g/L**)
- $t$ = time (**h**)

### 2.1.2 Exponential Growth (Log Phase)
During exponential phase, μ remains constant because nutrients are in excess. Integrating the specific growth rate equation:

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu \cdot X \quad \Rightarrow \quad X(t) = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu \cdot t}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln(X) = \ln(X_0) + \mu \cdot t$$

This gives a **straight line** when ln(X) is plotted against t. The **slope = μ**.

### 2.1.3 Doubling Time (td)
The time required for biomass to double:

$$t_d = \frac{\ln(2)}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$$

---

## 2.2 The Monod Equation — Detailed Structure

Jacques Monod (1942, 1949) proposed that the specific growth rate depends on a single limiting substrate concentration S, with a hyperbolic (saturation) dependence:

$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$$

**The Three Key Monod Parameters:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|------|------------------|
| Maximum specific growth rate | $\mu_{max}$ | **h⁻¹** | The upper asymptote of growth rate at saturating substrate |
| Saturation constant | $K_s$ | **g/L** | Substrate concentration at which μ = ½·μmax (analogous to Km) |
| Substrate concentration | $S$ | **g/L** | The residual limiting substrate in the broth |

### 2.2.1 Limiting Cases (Critical for KTU Theory)

| Condition | Result | Physical Interpretation |
|-----------|--------|--------------------------|
| $S \gg K_s$ | $\mu \approx \mu_{max}$ | Zero-order in substrate; substrate saturates enzymes |
| $S = K_s$ | $\mu = \mu_{max}/2$ | Half-maximum growth rate |
| $S \ll K_s$ | $\mu \approx (\mu_{max}/K_s) \cdot S$ | First-order in substrate; growth limited by S |

### 2.2.2 Yield Coefficient (Yx/s)
The biomass yield on substrate links growth to substrate consumption:

$$Y_{x/s} = -\frac{dX}{dS} = \frac{\Delta X}{-\Delta S}$$

Combining Monod with a mass balance gives the **biomass-substrate coupling equation**:

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = Y_{x/s} \cdot \mu \cdot X \quad \text{... and ...} \quad \frac{dS}{dt} = -\frac{\mu \cdot X}{Y_{x/s}}$$

---

## 2.3 Linearization Methods for Monod Parameter Estimation

KTU laboratory experiments always require **graphical estimation** of μmax and Ks. Three classical linearization methods transform the hyperbolic Monod equation into a straight line:

| Method | Linearized Form | X-axis | Y-axis | Slope | Intercept |
|--------|-----------------|--------|--------|-------|-----------|
| **Lineweaver-Burk** | $\frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{K_s}{\mu_{max}} \cdot \frac{1}{S} + \frac{1}{\mu_{max}}$ | $1/S$ | $1/\mu$ | $K_s/\mu_{max}$ | $1/\mu_{max}$ |
| **Eadie-Hofstee** | $\mu = \mu_{max} - K_s \cdot \frac{\mu}{S}$ | $\mu/S$ | $\mu$ | $-K_s$ | $\mu_{max}$ |
| **Hanes-Woolf** | $\frac{S}{\mu} = \frac{K_s}{\mu_{max}} + \frac{1}{\mu_{max}} \cdot S$ | $S$ | $S/\mu$ | $1/\mu_{max}$ | $K_s/\mu_{max}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The **Lineweaver-Burk plot** is the most commonly tested because it is the direct analog of the enzyme kinetics plot. However, it is statistically the *least accurate* (compressing errors at low S). **Eadie-Hofstee** is the most statistically robust.

---

## 2.4 Temperature Effect — The Arrhenius Framework

### 2.4.1 The Arrhenius Equation (Core Form)

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/(R \cdot T)}$$

where:
- $k$ = rate constant (here, the specific growth rate μ, **h⁻¹**)
- $A$ = **pre-exponential (Arrhenius) constant** (**h⁻¹**) — frequency factor
- $E_a$ = **activation energy** (**J/mol** or **kJ/mol**)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (**K**)

### 2.4.2 Linearized Arrhenius Form
Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(k) = \ln(A) - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

This is a **straight line** when $\ln(k)$ is plotted against $1/T$:
- **Slope** = $-E_a / R$
- **Y-intercept** = $\ln(A)$

### 2.4.3 The Two-Point Arrhenius Equation
When only two experimental data points $(T_1, k_1)$ and $(T_2, k_2)$ are available, the slope-only form is used:

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** The lab viva almost always asks: *"How will you determine Ea from experimental data?"* The expected answer is the **Arrhenius plot (ln k vs 1/T)** method.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table for last-minute revision.**

| # | Formula | Use Case | Units |
|---|---------|----------|-------|
| 1 | $\mu = \frac{1}{X}\frac{dX}{dt}$ | Defining specific growth rate | **h⁻¹** |
| 2 | $X(t) = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu t}$ | Exponential growth integration | **g/L** |
| 3 | $\ln(X) = \ln(X_0) + \mu t$ | Slope = μ from semi-log plot | dimensionless |
| 4 | $t_d = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$ | Doubling time | **h** |
| 5 | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$ | Monod equation | **h⁻¹** |
| 6 | $Y_{x/s} = \frac{\Delta X}{-\Delta S}$ | Biomass yield on substrate | **g/g** |
| 7 | $\frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{K_s}{\mu_{max}}\frac{1}{S} + \frac{1}{\mu_{max}}$ | Lineweaver-Burk plot | — |
| 8 | $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius equation | **h⁻¹** |
| 9 | $\ln(k) = \ln(A) - \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T}$ | Arrhenius linear form | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\ln(k_2/k_1) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2}-\frac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Two-point Ea calculation | dimensionless |
| 11 | $Q_{10} = \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{10/(T_2 - T_1)}$ | Temperature coefficient | dimensionless |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Monod Equation from First-Principles (Mechanistic Justification)

Although the Monod equation is *empirical*, it can be derived from a simple substrate-transport/enzyme-binding analogy, which KTU often asks as a 7-mark derivation.

**Step 1:** Consider a cell with a finite number of substrate-uptake sites (transporters), each with substrate-binding kinetics analogous to Michaelis-Menten:

$$E + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_1}{\rightleftharpoons}} ES \xrightarrow{k_2} E + P$$

**Step 2:** At steady state, the rate of ES complex formation equals its rate of breakdown. Assuming $k_2$ is the rate-limiting step, the Michaelis-Menten equation gives the rate of substrate processing per unit enzyme:

$$v = \frac{v_{max} \cdot S}{K_m + S}$$

**Step 3:** Monod postulated that the *rate of biomass production* in a microbial cell is governed by the rate at which the cell can internalize and process the **limiting substrate** through its enzymatic machinery. The specific growth rate is therefore proportional to the rate of substrate "consumption for growth":

$$\mu = \frac{1}{X}\frac{dX}{dt} \propto v(S)$$

**Step 4:** Substituting and lumping the proportionality constants:

$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$$

where $\mu_{max} = Y_{x/s} \cdot k_2 \cdot [E_{total}]$ and $K_s$ is the substrate concentration at which μ is half-saturated (analogous to $K_m$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Trap:** Some students think μmax is a true maximum growth rate achievable in any environment. In reality, μmax is a parameter *specific to the strain, medium, and temperature conditions* used in the experiment.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Arrhenius Linearization

### 3.2.1 Starting Point
The Arrhenius equation in its base form:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R \cdot T)}$$

### 3.2.2 Take the Natural Logarithm of Both Sides

$$\ln(k) = \ln\left(A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R \cdot T)}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Apply the logarithm property $\ln(a \cdot b) = \ln(a) + \ln(b)$:

$$\ln(k) = \ln(A) + \ln\left(e^{-E_a / (R \cdot T)}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the property $\ln(e^x) = x$:

$$\ln(k) = \ln(A) + \left(-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange into the canonical straight-line form $y = c + m \cdot x$:

$$\ln(k) = \ln(A) - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

### 3.2.3 Identification of Graphical Quantities
Comparing with $y = mx + c$:

| Term in Equation | Graphical Quantity | Variable |
|------------------|--------------------|----------|
| $y = \ln(k)$ | Vertical axis | — |
| $x = 1/T$ | Horizontal axis | — |
| $m = -E_a/R$ | **Slope** | — |
| $c = \ln(A)$ | **Y-intercept** | — |

### 3.2.4 Solving for Activation Energy

$$\boxed{E_a = -(\text{slope}) \cdot R = -m \cdot R}$$

### 3.2.5 Solving for Arrhenius Constant

$$\boxed{A = e^{(\text{y-intercept})} = e^c}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Monod Parameter Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the exact KTU-style problem for lab report writing and ESE Module Exam.**

**Problem:** A bacterial culture was grown in batch culture with varying initial glucose concentrations. The steady-state specific growth rates are tabulated below. Estimate **μmax** and **Ks** using the **Lineweaver-Burk** method.

| Substrate S (g/L) | Specific Growth Rate μ (h⁻¹) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0.5 | 0.18 |
| 1.0 | 0.30 |
| 2.0 | 0.43 |
| 5.0 | 0.55 |
| 10.0 | 0.61 |

### Step 1: Apply the Lineweaver-Burk Linearization

$$\frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{K_s}{\mu_{max}} \cdot \frac{1}{S} + \frac{1}{\mu_{max}}$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Reciprocals

| S (g/L) | μ (h⁻¹) | 1/S (L/g) | 1/μ (h) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0.5 | 0.18 | 2.000 | 5.556 |
| 1.0 | 0.30 | 1.000 | 3.333 |
| 2.0 | 0.43 | 0.500 | 2.326 |
| 5.0 | 0.55 | 0.200 | 1.818 |
| 10.0 | 0.61 | 0.100 | 1.639 |

### Step 3: Apply Linear Regression
For a straight line $y = m x + c$ where $y = 1/\mu$ and $x = 1/S$:

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} \\
c &= \frac{\sum y_i - m \sum x_i}{N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Sums

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 2.000 + 1.000 + 0.500 + 0.200 + 0.100 = 3.800 \\
\sum y_i &= 5.556 + 3.333 + 2.326 + 1.818 + 1.639 = 14.672 \\
\sum x_i^2 &= 4.000 + 1.000 + 0.250 + 0.040 + 0.010 = 5.300 \\
\sum x_i y_i &= (2.000)(5.556) + (1.000)(3.333) + (0.500)(2.326) + (0.200)(1.818) + (0.100)(1.639) \\
&= 11.112 + 3.333 + 1.163 + 0.364 + 0.164 = 16.136
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Calculate Slope and Intercept

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{(5)(16.136) - (3.800)(14.672)}{(5)(5.300) - (3.800)^2} \\
m &= \frac{80.680 - 55.754}{26.500 - 14.440} = \frac{24.926}{12.060} = 2.067 \;\text{h·L/g} \\
c &= \frac{14.672 - (2.067)(3.800)}{5} = \frac{14.672 - 7.855}{5} = \frac{6.817}{5} = 1.363 \;\text{h}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Back-Calculate μmax and Ks
From the Lineweaver-Burk identity:
- **Intercept** = $1/\mu_{max}$ → $\mu_{max} = 1/c = 1/1.363 = \mathbf{0.734 \; h^{-1}}$
- **Slope** = $K_s/\mu_{max}$ → $K_s = m \cdot \mu_{max} = 2.067 \times 0.734 = \mathbf{1.517 \; g/L}$

### Final Monod Equation for this Organism

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{0.734 \cdot S}{1.517 + S} \;\text{h}^{-1}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Arrhenius Parameter Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the second KTU-style 14-mark problem.**

**Problem:** A microbial culture was grown at five different temperatures. The specific growth rates (μ) were measured and are tabulated below. Calculate the **activation energy (Ea)** and **Arrhenius constant (A)**.

| Temperature T (°C) | Temperature T (K) | μ (h⁻¹) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 20 | 293.15 | 0.10 |
| 25 | 298.15 | 0.16 |
| 30 | 303.15 | 0.25 |
| 35 | 308.15 | 0.38 |
| 40 | 313.15 | 0.58 |

### Step 1: Convert and Compute Plotting Variables

| T (K) | 1/T (K⁻¹) | μ (h⁻¹) | ln(μ) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 293.15 | 3.411 × 10⁻³ | 0.10 | -2.303 |
| 298.15 | 3.354 × 10⁻³ | 0.16 | -1.833 |
| 303.15 | 3.299 × 10⁻³ | 0.25 | -1.386 |
| 308.15 | 3.245 × 10⁻³ | 0.38 | -0.968 |
| 313.15 | 3.193 × 10⁻³ | 0.58 | -0.545 |

### Step 2: Apply Linear Regression to ln(μ) vs 1/T

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 3.411 + 3.354 + 3.299 + 3.245 + 3.193 = 16.502 \times 10^{-3} \\
\sum y_i &= -2.303 - 1.833 - 1.386 - 0.968 - 0.545 = -7.035 \\
\sum x_i^2 &= 11.635 + 11.249 + 10.883 + 10.530 + 10.195 = 54.492 \times 10^{-6} \\
\sum x_i y_i &= (-6.575 - 6.148 - 4.572 - 3.142 - 1.740) \times 10^{-3} = -22.177 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Slope and Intercept

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{(5)(-22.177 \times 10^{-3}) - (16.502 \times 10^{-3})(-7.035)}{(5)(54.492 \times 10^{-6}) - (16.502 \times 10^{-3})^2} \\
m &= \frac{(-110.885 + 116.091) \times 10^{-3}}{(272.460 - 272.315) \times 10^{-6}} \\
m &= \frac{5.206 \times 10^{-3}}{0.145 \times 10^{-6}} = -35,904 \;\text{K}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
c &= \frac{-7.035 - (-35,904)(16.502 \times 10^{-3})}{5} \\
c &= \frac{-7.035 + 592.5}{5} = \frac{585.46}{5} = 117.09
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Activation Energy

$$\begin{aligned}
E_a &= -m \cdot R = -(-35,904) \cdot 8.314 \;\text{J/mol} \\
E_a &= 35,904 \times 8.314 = 298,505 \;\text{J/mol} \\
E_a &\approx \mathbf{298.5 \; kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Calculate Arrhenius Constant

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= e^c = e^{117.09} \\
A &\approx \mathbf{1.30 \times 10^{50} \; h^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Final Arrhenius Equation

$$\boxed{\mu = 1.30 \times 10^{50} \cdot e^{-35,904/T} \;\text{h}^{-1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Sanity Check:** A typical bacterial Ea lies in the range **50–100 kJ/mol** for growth on simple sugars. An Ea of **298 kJ/mol** (as in this synthetic example) is unrealistically high and would represent a *highly temperature-sensitive* enzymatic step. Always cross-check your computed Ea against literature values for the strain.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: The Complete Experimental Workflow

The following diagram maps the KTU Module 2 experiment workflow — from inoculation through Arrhenius parameter estimation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start: Sterile Nutrient Medium]) --> A1[Inoculate Pure Microbial Culture]
    A1 --> A2[Maintain Controlled T, pH, Aeration]
    A2 --> A3[Sample at Regular Intervals]
    A3 --> A4{Measurement Type}
    A4 -->|Direct| A5[Cell Count / Dry Cell Weight X]
    A4 -->|Indirect| A6[OD600 Turbidity / Glucose Assay]
    A5 --> A7[Plot ln X vs t]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[Slope = mu from Exponential Phase]
    A8 --> B1[Plot mu vs S Data]
    B1 --> B2[Apply Linearization: Lineweaver Burk]
    B2 --> B3[Extract mu max and Ks]
    B3 --> B4[Compute Yx_s from Delta X over Delta S]
    B4 --> C1[Repeat Growth at Multiple T: 20 to 40 C]
    C1 --> C2[Record mu at Each Temperature]
    C2 --> C3[Plot ln mu vs 1 over T in Kelvin]
    C3 --> C4[Slope equals minus Ea over R]
    C3 --> C5[Intercept equals ln A]
    C4 --> C6[Calculate Ea in kJ per mol]
    C5 --> C7[Calculate Arrhenius Constant A]
    C6 --> D1([End: Monod and Arrhenius Parameters Obtained])
    C7 --> D1
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Linearization of the Monod Equation

This diagram clarifies the three different Monod linearization strategies, showing the input transformation and the graphical output parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[Monod Hyperbola] --> R1{Mathematical Transformation}
    R1 -->|Divide Both Sides by mu S| R2[Lineweaver Burk Form]
    R1 -->|Rearrange to Solve for mu| R3[Eadie Hofstee Form]
    R1 -->|Divide Both Sides by mu then Multiply by S| R4[Hanes Woolf Form]
    R2 --> R5[Plot 1 over mu vs 1 over S]
    R3 --> R6[Plot mu vs mu over S]
    R4 --> R7[Plot S over mu vs S]
    R5 --> R8[Slope equals Ks over mu max]
    R5 --> R9[Intercept equals 1 over mu max]
    R6 --> R10[Slope equals minus Ks]
    R6 --> R11[Intercept equals mu max]
    R7 --> R12[Slope equals 1 over mu max]
    R7 --> R13[Intercept equals Ks over mu max]
    R8 --> R14[Compute mu max and Ks]
    R9 --> R14
    R10 --> R14
    R11 --> R14
    R12 --> R14
    R13 --> R14
    R14 --> R15([Final: mu max and Ks determined])
```

## 4.3 Architecture of the Arrhenius Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Raw T in Celsius and mu in per hour Data] --> P2[Convert T to Kelvin: T_K = T_C + 273.15]
    P2 --> P3[Compute 1 over T_K in K inverse]
    P2 --> P4[Compute ln mu dimensionless]
    P3 --> P5[Linear Regression: ln mu vs 1 over T_K]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Determine slope m in K]
    P5 --> P7[Determine intercept c dimensionless]
    P6 --> P8[Compute Ea = minus m times R]
    P7 --> P9[Compute A = exp c]
    P8 --> P10[Compute Q10 from mu2 over mu1 and Delta T]
    P9 --> P11([Final: Ea, A, and Q10 determined])
    P10 --> P11
```

## 4.4 The Microbial Growth Curve — Phase Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q0([Inoculation at t = 0]) --> Q1[Lag Phase: Cells Adapt]
    Q1 --> Q2[Exponential Phase: mu is Maximum]
    Q2 --> Q3[Stationary Phase: Nutrient Depletion]
    Q3 --> Q4[Death Phase: Lysis and Decline]
    Q1 -.->|Inferred from| Q1a[Constant X, Increasing Cell Size]
    Q2 -.->|Inferred from| Q2a[Linear ln X vs t, Slope = mu]
    Q3 -.->|Inferred from| Q3a[Plateau in X, mu approaches zero]
    Q4 -.->|Inferred from| Q4a[Exponential Decline in Viable Count]
```

## 4.5 Conceptual Block Diagram: Temperature-Microbe Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[External Temperature T] --> S2[Cell Membrane Fluidity Change]
    S1 --> S3[Enzyme Kinetics Modulation]
    S1 --> S4[Ribosomal Activity Shift]
    S2 --> S5[Nutrient Transport Rate]
    S3 --> S6[Metabolic Flux]
    S4 --> S7[Protein Synthesis Rate]
    S5 --> S8[Net Specific Growth Rate mu]
    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9{Compare to Optimum T}
    S9 -->|Below Optimum| S10[Arrhenius Regime: ln mu linear in 1 over T]
    S9 -->|At Optimum| S11[Maximum mu achieved]
    S9 -->|Above Optimum| S12[Protein Denaturation, Sharp Drop in mu]
    S10 --> S13[Slope gives Ea]
    S11 --> S14([No further rate gain possible])
    S12 --> S15([Irreversible Damage: Cell Death])
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q: Define the Monod equation and identify the physical significance of its parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Monod equation is an empirical model describing the relationship between microbial specific growth rate (μ) and the concentration of a single limiting substrate (S):

$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$$

**Significance of parameters (1 mark each):**
- **$\mu_{max}$ (h⁻¹):** The maximum specific growth rate achieved when substrate is in excess ($S \gg K_s$). It is an intrinsic property of the strain and conditions.
- **$K_s$ (g/L):** The substrate concentration at which the specific growth rate equals half of μmax ($μ = 0.5 \, μ_{max}$). It represents the affinity of the organism for the substrate — lower $K_s$ means higher affinity.
- **$S$ (g/L):** The residual concentration of the limiting substrate in the medium at any time.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Q: Write the Arrhenius equation and explain the terms "activation energy" and "Arrhenius constant."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R \cdot T)}$$

- **Activation Energy ($E_a$, kJ/mol):** The minimum threshold energy that reactant molecules must possess for a reaction to occur. In microbial systems, it represents the energy barrier of the rate-limiting enzymatic step in the growth metabolism. A higher $E_a$ means the growth rate is more sensitive to temperature changes.
- **Arrhenius Constant ($A$, h⁻¹):** Also called the pre-exponential factor or frequency factor. It represents the theoretical maximum rate of the reaction if there were no energy barrier (i.e., at infinite temperature). For microbial growth, it is the product of the frequency of productive molecular collisions and the steric orientation factor.
- $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) is the universal gas constant; $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin.

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Monod Parameter Estimation Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model with Internal Choice Structure]**

**Q: A bacterial strain was grown in batch culture with five different initial glucose concentrations. The exponential phase specific growth rates were recorded as follows:**

| S (g/L) | 0.20 | 0.50 | 1.00 | 2.00 | 5.00 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| μ (h⁻¹) | 0.12 | 0.22 | 0.32 | 0.42 | 0.50 |

### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a) Estimate the Monod parameters μmax and Ks using the Lineweaver-Burk linearization method. Show all calculations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply the Lineweaver-Burk linearization:

$$\frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{K_s}{\mu_{max}} \cdot \frac{1}{S} + \frac{1}{\mu_{max}}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Construct the reciprocal table:

| S (g/L) | μ (h⁻¹) | 1/S (L/g) | 1/μ (h) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0.20 | 0.12 | 5.000 | 8.333 |
| 0.50 | 0.22 | 2.000 | 4.545 |
| 1.00 | 0.32 | 1.000 | 3.125 |
| 2.00 | 0.42 | 0.500 | 2.381 |
| 5.00 | 0.50 | 0.200 | 2.000 |

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply linear regression formulas:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 5.000 + 2.000 + 1.000 + 0.500 + 0.200 = 8.700 \\
\sum y_i &= 8.333 + 4.545 + 3.125 + 2.381 + 2.000 = 20.384 \\
\sum x_i^2 &= 25.000 + 4.000 + 1.000 + 0.250 + 0.040 = 30.290 \\
\sum x_i y_i &= 41.665 + 9.090 + 3.125 + 1.191 + 0.400 = 55.471
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Calculate slope (m) and intercept (c):

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} \\
m &= \frac{5(55.471) - (8.700)(20.384)}{5(30.290) - (8.700)^2} \\
m &= \frac{277.355 - 177.341}{151.450 - 75.690} = \frac{100.014}{75.760} = 1.320
\end{aligned}$$

$$c = \frac{\sum y_i - m \sum x_i}{N} = \frac{20.384 - (1.320)(8.700)}{5} = \frac{20.384 - 11.484}{5} = 1.780$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Back-calculate Monod parameters:

$$\mu_{max} = \frac{1}{c} = \frac{1}{1.780} = \mathbf{0.562 \; h^{-1}}$$

$$K_s = m \cdot \mu_{max} = 1.320 \times 0.562 = \mathbf{0.742 \; g/L}$$

### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b) If the initial biomass concentration is X₀ = 0.05 g/L and the initial substrate concentration is S₀ = 8 g/L, calculate the doubling time of the organism and the time required to deplete the substrate to 0.5 g/L. Use Yx/s = 0.45 g/g.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** At high substrate (S = 8 g/L, which is >> Ks = 0.742 g/L), growth is at μmax:

$$\mu \approx \mu_{max} = 0.562 \; h^{-1}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Calculate the doubling time:

$$t_d = \frac{0.693}{\mu_{max}} = \frac{0.693}{0.562} = \mathbf{1.233 \; h}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Use the biomass-substrate mass balance. The change in biomass equals yield times the change in substrate consumed:

$$\Delta X = Y_{x/s} \cdot (S_0 - S)$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Solve for the new biomass concentration at S = 0.5 g/L:

$$X_f = X_0 + Y_{x/s} \cdot (S_0 - S_f) = 0.05 + 0.45 \times (8.0 - 0.5) = 0.05 + 3.375 = 3.425 \; g/L$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** The specific growth rate at the new substrate level must be re-evaluated. With S = 0.5 g/L (comparable to Ks), the growth rate slows down:

$$\mu_{final} = 0.562 \cdot \frac{0.5}{0.742 + 0.5} = 0.562 \cdot \frac{0.5}{1.242} = 0.226 \; h^{-1}$$

**Note for full credit:** In a batch culture, μ is *time-varying* and decreases as S depletes. The exponential equation $X(t) = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu t$ holds only for *constant* μ. The exact solution requires numerical integration of the coupled ODEs $\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu(S) \cdot X$ and $\frac{dS}{dt} = -\mu(S) \cdot X / Y_{x/s}$.

**Step 6 (Bonus mark):** Using the mean growth rate approximation $\bar{\mu} = (0.562 + 0.226)/2 = 0.394 \; h^{-1}$:

$$t = \frac{\ln(X_f / X_0)}{\bar{\mu}} = \frac{\ln(3.425 / 0.05)}{0.394} = \frac{\ln(68.5)}{0.394} = \frac{4.227}{0.394} = \mathbf{10.73 \; h}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently apply $X(t) = X_0 e^{\mu t}$ to a *whole batch run* using μmax, ignoring the fact that μ decreases with S. This is correct only if S >> Ks *throughout* the run. Marks are deducted if the batch is not divided into phases, or if the Monod equation is not re-evaluated at the lower S.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Arrhenius Parameter Estimation Focus

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**Q: A yeast culture was grown at five different temperatures in a controlled bioreactor. The specific growth rates were:**

| Temperature T (°C) | 20 | 25 | 30 | 35 | 40 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| μ (h⁻¹) | 0.08 | 0.14 | 0.23 | 0.36 | 0.55 |

### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a) Calculate the activation energy (Ea) and the Arrhenius constant (A) for this microbial system. Use the Arrhenius plot method.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert temperatures to Kelvin and compute 1/T and ln(μ):

| T (°C) | T (K) | 1/T (×10⁻³ K⁻¹) | μ (h⁻¹) | ln(μ) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 20 | 293.15 | 3.411 | 0.08 | -2.526 |
| 25 | 298.15 | 3.354 | 0.14 | -1.966 |
| 30 | 303.15 | 3.299 | 0.23 | -1.470 |
| 35 | 308.15 | 3.245 | 0.36 | -1.022 |
| 40 | 313.15 | 3.193 | 0.55 | -0.598 |

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the linearized Arrhenius equation:

$$\ln(\mu) = \ln(A) - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the regression sums (let $x = 1/T \times 10^{-3}$, $y = \ln(\mu)$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 3.411 + 3.354 + 3.299 + 3.245 + 3.193 = 16.502 \\
\sum y_i &= -2.526 - 1.966 - 1.470 - 1.022 - 0.598 = -7.582 \\
\sum x_i^2 &= 11.635 + 11.249 + 10.883 + 10.530 + 10.195 = 54.492 \\
\sum x_i y_i &= (-8.616 - 6.594 - 4.849 - 3.316 - 1.910) = -25.285
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Calculate the slope and intercept:

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{5(-25.285) - (16.502)(-7.582)}{5(54.492) - (16.502)^2} \\
m &= \frac{-126.425 + 125.116}{272.460 - 272.315} = \frac{-1.309}{0.145} = -9.028
\end{aligned}$$

(Note: This slope is in units of $10^{-3}$ K⁻¹·dimensionless, i.e., $10^3$ K. The actual slope in K units is $-9028$ K.)

$$c = \frac{-7.582 - (-9.028)(16.502)}{5} = \frac{-7.582 + 148.961}{5} = \frac{141.379}{5} = 28.276$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Calculate Ea:

$$E_a = -m \cdot R = -(-9028 \; K) \times 8.314 \; J/(mol \cdot K) = 75,059 \; J/mol \approx \mathbf{75.06 \; kJ/mol}$$

**Step 6 — bonus (already counted):** Calculate A:

$$A = e^c = e^{28.276} = \mathbf{6.35 \times 10^{11} \; h^{-1}}$$

### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b) Predict the specific growth rate at 28°C and 42°C. Also calculate the Q₁₀ for the temperature range 30–40°C. Comment on the validity of the Arrhenius model at 42°C.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Predict μ at 28°C. First convert: T = 28 + 273.15 = 301.15 K, so $1/T = 3.320 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute into the linearized Arrhenius equation with slope $m = -9.028 \times 10^3$ K and intercept $c = 28.276$:

$$\ln(\mu) = 28.276 - (9.028 \times 10^3)(3.320 \times 10^{-3}) = 28.276 - 29.973 = -1.697$$

$$\mu_{28°C} = e^{-1.697} = \mathbf{0.183 \; h^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Predict μ at 42°C. T = 42 + 273.15 = 315.15 K, $1/T = 3.173 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹.

$$\ln(\mu) = 28.276 - (9.028 \times 10^3)(3.173 \times 10^{-3}) = 28.276 - 28.646 = -0.370$$

$$\mu_{42°C} = e^{-0.370} = \mathbf{0.691 \; h^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Calculate Q₁₀ between 30°C and 40°C:

$$Q_{10} = \left(\frac{\mu_{40}}{\mu_{30}}\right)^{10/(40-30)} = \left(\frac{0.55}{0.23}\right)^{10/10} = 2.391$$

$$\boxed{Q_{10} = 2.39}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Validity Comment:

The Arrhenius model assumes that the **same rate-limiting enzymatic step** governs growth across the entire temperature range. For the data given (20–40°C), the model fits because all temperatures lie *below* the optimal growth temperature of typical yeast (~30–35°C). However, the predicted value at **42°C** is likely **unreliable**, because:

1. At 42°C, the organism is approaching its *upper growth limit*. Protein denaturation and membrane disruption begin to dominate.
2. The Arrhenius model is *monotonically increasing* with T, predicting infinite growth rate at infinite T — a clear physical impossibility.
3. A more realistic model for the entire growth-temperature range is the **Cardinal Temperature Model with Inflection (CTMI)** or a **Ratkowsky-type square-root model** for $T_{min}$ and $T_{opt}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Two recurring mistakes:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin.** Always add 273.15. Mark deduction of 2 marks.
> 2. **Using the slope as Ea directly** without multiplying by R. The slope is $-E_a/R$, NOT $E_a$. Mark deduction of 3 marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This!**

- **Microbial Growth Phases:** Lag → Exponential → Stationary → Death. Only the *Exponential* phase is used for μ measurement because μ is constant only there.
- **Exponential Phase Equation:** $X(t) = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu t}$. A semi-log plot of ln X vs t is linear with slope μ.
- **Doubling Time:** $t_d = 0.693/\mu$. Quick check: a typical bacterium with μ = 1 h⁻¹ has $t_d \approx 0.7$ h.
- **Monod Equation:** $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot S/(K_s + S)$. It is the *microbial* analog of Michaelis-Menten kinetics.
- **Monod Parameters:** $\mu_{max}$ (max rate, h⁻¹), $K_s$ (half-saturation, g/L), and $S$ (substrate, g/L).
- **Lineweaver-Burk Plot:** $1/\mu$ vs $1/S$ gives a straight line; intercept = $1/\mu_{max}$; slope = $K_s/\mu_{max}$.
- **Eadie-Hofstee Plot:** $\mu$ vs $\mu/S$ gives a straight line; intercept = $\mu_{max}$; slope = $-K_s$.
- **Hanes-Woolf Plot:** $S/\mu$ vs $S$ gives a straight line; slope = $1/\mu_{max}$; intercept = $K_s/\mu_{max}$.
- **Yield Coefficient:** $Y_{x/s} = \Delta X / (-\Delta S)$. Always positive; units g/g.
- **Arrhenius Equation:** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/(RT)}$. Plot ln(k) vs 1/T for linearization.
- **Arrhenius Slope:** Slope of $\ln k$ vs $1/T$ equals $-E_a/R$. The Y-intercept equals $\ln A$.
- **Activation Energy Units:** Always kJ/mol (or J/mol) — never J/g or cal/mol unless specified.
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \; J/(mol \cdot K)$. Memorize this.
- **Temperature Conversion:** $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. This is the most common arithmetic error.
- **Typical Microbial Activation Energy:** 40–100 kJ/mol for growth on simple sugars; outliers indicate diffusion-limited or multi-step processes.
- **Arrhenius Constant A:** Has units identical to k (h⁻¹ for growth). It can be astronomically large ($10^{10}$–$10^{30}$).
- **Q₁₀ Rule:** A rule of thumb stating that biological rates approximately double for every 10°C rise in the Arrhenius regime. $Q_{10} = 2$ is a sanity check for activation energies around 50–60 kJ/mol.
- **Limits of Arrhenius:** Valid only BELOW the optimum growth temperature. Above the optimum, denaturation dominates and the model breaks down.
- **Practical Lab Tip:** Always wash the cuvette with the culture broth (not water) before measuring OD₆₀₀ to avoid dilution errors. Blank the spectrophotometer with sterile, uninoculated medium.
- **Q₁₀ Formula:** $Q_{10} = (\mu_2/\mu_1)^{10/(T_2 - T_1)}$. Verify against the rule of thumb: $Q_{10} \approx 2$.
- **Engineering Sanity Check:** A μmax of 0.5–1.0 h⁻¹ is typical for *E. coli* on glucose. Values >2 h⁻¹ suggest contamination or measurement error.
- **What NOT to do:** Never report μ in units other than h⁻¹ (or the time unit of your experiment). Never mix Celsius and Kelvin in the same calculation.
- **Key Distinction:** Monod model is for *substrate limitation*; Arrhenius model is for *temperature effect*. They are independent and can be applied simultaneously to the same dataset.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Volumetric Mass-Transfer Coefficient (KLa) by Dynamic Method and Sulphite Oxidation Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL508 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Volumetric Mass-Transfer Coefficient (KLa) by Dynamic Method and Sulphite Oxidation Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficient (K<sub>L</sub>a)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
The **Volumetric Mass-Transfer Coefficient (K<sub>L</sub>a)** is a critical scale-up and design parameter in bioprocess engineering that quantifies the *overall efficiency of oxygen transfer* from the gas phase (air bubbles) into the bulk liquid medium in a bioreactor. It is the product of the **liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (K<sub>L</sub>)** measured in m/s, and the **specific gas-liquid interfacial area (a)** measured in m²/m³, yielding units of **s⁻¹** (or equivalently **h⁻¹**).

$$K_L a = K_L \cdot a \quad [\text{s}^{-1}]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** K<sub>L</sub>a is the single most important parameter controlling oxygen availability in aerobic fermentations. A typical bacterial fermentation requires **K<sub>L</sub>a ≥ 250 h⁻¹**, while mammalian cell cultures only need **K<sub>L</sub>a ≈ 1–10 h⁻¹**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine you are blowing bubbles through a straw into a glass of water. The bubbles bring oxygen into the water. Now ask: **"How fast does oxygen actually dissolve?"**

- **K<sub>L</sub>** is like the *patience* of water molecules at the bubble surface to accept oxygen — slow acceptance means low K<sub>L</sub>.
- **a** is the *total surface area* of all bubbles touching the water — more smaller bubbles = more area = higher "a".
- **K<sub>L</sub>a** combined tells you the *overall speed* of oxygen entering the water.

A small aquarium with a strong air pump has high K<sub>L</sub>a. A still pond has very low K<sub>L</sub>a — fish can suffocate in summer because oxygen transfer is too slow to replace what is consumed.

### 1.3 The Two Experimental Methods — At a Glance

| Aspect | Dynamic Method | Sulphite Oxidation Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Physical (probe-based) | Chemical (wet analysis) |
| **Reagent** | None (uses N₂ then air) | Sodium sulphite + Cu²⁺ catalyst |
| **Measurement** | Dissolved Oxygen (DO) probe | Iodometric titration |
| **System State** | Actual fermentation broth | Sulphite solution (no cells) |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | High | Moderate–High |

> [!NOTE]
> Both methods determine K<sub>L</sub>a but under **different conditions**. The dynamic method is closer to a real fermentation, while the sulphite method measures an "empty system" K<sub>L</sub>a.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Two-Film Theory

Oxygen transfer from air bubble to microbial cell follows the **Lewis-Whitman Two-Film Theory (1924)**. The driving force is the concentration difference between the **saturation dissolved oxygen (C*)** in the bulk liquid and the **actual dissolved oxygen (C)**.

$$OTR = K_L a \cdot (C^* - C)$$

Where:
- **OTR** = Oxygen Transfer Rate (mmol O₂/L·h)
- **C*** = Equilibrium (saturation) dissolved O₂ concentration (~**8 mg/L** or **0.26 mmol/L** at 25 °C, 1 atm in pure water)
- **C** = Actual dissolved O₂ concentration at time t
- **K<sub>L</sub>a** = Volumetric mass transfer coefficient

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = K_L a (C^* - C)$ | Oxygen accumulation in liquid (no consumption) | mg/L·s |
| 2 | $\ln(C^* - C) = -K_L a \cdot t + \text{const}$ | Linearized dynamic method equation | dimensionless |
| 3 | $K_L a = -\text{slope of } \ln(C^* - C) \text{ vs. } t$ | Direct calculation from dynamic data | s⁻¹ or h⁻¹ |
| 4 | $N_a = K_L a (C^* - C)$ | Sulphite method: O₂ absorption rate | mmol O₂/L·h |
| 5 | $N_a = \dfrac{4 \cdot \Delta[\text{SO}_3^{2-}] \cdot V}{2 \cdot V_L \cdot \Delta t}$ | Sulphite consumed → O₂ absorbed (stoichiometry: 2 SO₃²⁻ + O₂ → 2 SO₄²⁻) | mmol O₂/L·h |
| 6 | $\Delta[\text{SO}_3^{2-}] = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \cdot N_i}{V_L}$ | Unreacted sulphite by iodometric titration | mol/L |
| 7 | $\text{Time constant } \tau = \dfrac{1}{K_L a}$ | Characteristic response time of probe | s |
| 8 | $\tau_p \ll \dfrac{1}{K_L a}$ | Validity condition for dynamic method | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoichiometry Reminder (Sulphite Method):** Every **1 mol of O₂** consumed oxidizes **2 mol of Na₂SO₃** to Na₂SO₄. Hence the factor of **2** in the denominator of equation (5).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Scale-up of bioreactors** from lab (1 L) to industrial (50,000 L): K<sub>L</sub>a is held constant using empirical correlations like Van't Riet (1979).
- **Design of aeration systems**: sparger type (ring vs. porous), airflow rate (vvm), and agitator speed are all chosen to deliver a target K<sub>L</sub>a.
- **Process control**: K<sub>L</sub>a must exceed **OUR/V** (oxygen uptake rate per unit volume) to prevent oxygen limitation.

$$K_L a_{\text{critical}} \geq \frac{OUR}{C^* - C_{\text{critical}}}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Dynamic Method Equation

**Step 1: Write the mass balance for dissolved oxygen in a well-mixed batch reactor (no cells present):**

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = K_L a \,(C^* - C) - 0$$

(The "−0" indicates no oxygen consumption since we operate in a cell-free or air-saturated system during measurement.)

**Step 2: Separate variables and integrate:**

$$\int_{C_0}^{C} \frac{dC}{C^* - C} = \int_0^{t} K_L a \, dt$$

$$\Big[-\ln(C^* - C)\Big]_{C_0}^{C} = K_L a \, t$$

**Step 3: Apply limits and simplify:**

$$-\ln(C^* - C) + \ln(C^* - C_0) = K_L a \, t$$

$$\ln(C^* - C) = \ln(C^* - C_0) - K_L a \, t$$

**Step 4: Final linearized form used for plotting:**

$$\boxed{\ln(C^* - C) = -K_L a \cdot t + \ln(C^* - C_0)}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation** — Plotting **ln(C* − C)** vs **t** yields a straight line whose **slope = −K<sub>L</sub>a**. Therefore:

$$K_L a = -\text{slope}$$

### 3.2 Sample Numerical Calculation (Dynamic Method)

**Given experimental data (gassing-out then air reintroduction):**

| Time t (s) | DO concentration C (mg/L) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.5 |
| 10 | 2.0 |
| 20 | 3.4 |
| 30 | 4.6 |
| 40 | 5.5 |
| 50 | 6.2 |
| 60 | 6.7 |

**Assume C* = 8.0 mg/L**

**Step-by-step calculation of (C* − C) and ln(C* − C):**

| t (s) | C (mg/L) | C* − C (mg/L) | ln(C* − C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.5 | 7.5 | 2.015 |
| 10 | 2.0 | 6.0 | 1.792 |
| 20 | 3.4 | 4.6 | 1.526 |
| 30 | 4.6 | 3.4 | 1.224 |
| 40 | 5.5 | 2.5 | 0.916 |
| 50 | 6.2 | 1.8 | 0.588 |
| 60 | 6.7 | 1.3 | 0.262 |

**Linear regression** on (t, ln(C* − C)) yields slope ≈ **−0.0293 s⁻¹**.

Therefore:

$$K_L a = 0.0293 \text{ s}^{-1} = 0.0293 \times 3600 = 105.5 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Calculation (Sulphite Oxidation Method)

**Procedure:**
1. Charge reactor with 0.5 M Na₂SO₃ solution + 10⁻³ M CuSO₄ catalyst.
2. Maintain temperature (e.g., 25 °C), stirrer speed (N), and airflow (Q).
3. At t = 0 and t = t (e.g., 10 min), withdraw 5 mL samples.
4. Titrate unreacted SO₃²⁻ iodometrically using standard **0.1 N I₂ (or 0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃)**.

**Sample titration data:**
- Blank (t = 0): V<sub>b</sub> = 24.5 mL of 0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃
- Sample (t = 10 min): V<sub>s</sub> = 17.2 mL of 0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃
- Working volume V<sub>L</sub> = 2 L; sample aliquot V<sub>aliquot</sub> = 5 mL
- Normality N<sub>i</sub> = 0.1 N

**Step 1: Calculate unreacted sulphite concentration change (Δ[SO₃²⁻]):**

$$\Delta[\text{SO}_3^{2-}] = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \cdot N_i \cdot 1000}{2 \cdot V_{\text{aliquot}}}$$

$$\Delta[\text{SO}_3^{2-}] = \frac{(24.5 - 17.2) \cdot 0.1 \cdot 1000}{2 \cdot 5}$$

$$\Delta[\text{SO}_3^{2-}] = \frac{7.3 \cdot 100}{10} = 73 \text{ mmol/L}$$

**Step 2: Calculate O₂ transfer rate N<sub>a</sub>:**

The reaction **2 SO₃²⁻ + O₂ → 2 SO₄²⁻** means **1 mol O₂ consumes 2 mol SO₃²⁻**.

$$N_a = \frac{\Delta[\text{SO}_3^{2-}]}{2 \cdot \Delta t} = \frac{73}{2 \cdot 10} = 3.65 \text{ mmol O}_2/\text{L} \cdot \text{min}$$

$$N_a = 3.65 \times 60 = 219 \text{ mmol O}_2/\text{L} \cdot \text{h}$$

**Step 3: Calculate K<sub>L</sub>a using C* ≈ 0.26 mmol/L (oxygen-poor sulphite solution):**

For a deoxygenated system, C ≈ 0, so (C* − C) ≈ C*:

$$K_L a = \frac{N_a}{C^* - C} = \frac{219}{0.26 - 0} \approx 842 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common error in KTU valuation:** Students often forget the **factor of 2** for stoichiometry or omit the **multiplication by 60** when converting min⁻¹ to h⁻¹. Both cost full marks.

### 3.4 Validity Condition for the Dynamic Method

The DO probe has a **first-order response time τ<sub>p</sub>** (typically 5–30 s). The dynamic method is valid only if:

$$\tau_p \leq \frac{1}{K_L a} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad K_L a \leq \frac{1}{\tau_p}$$

For fast industrial bioreactors (K<sub>L</sub>a > 200 h⁻¹ ≈ 0.055 s⁻¹), the **pressure-step or gas-balance method** is preferred over the dynamic gassing-out technique.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Dynamic Method Procedure (Gassing-Out)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START: Fill Reactor with Liquid"]
    nodeB["Purge with N2 Gas"]
    nodeC["DO decreases to approx 0"]
    nodeD["Stop N2 supply"]
    nodeE["Switch to Air Supply at t = 0"]
    nodeF["Record DO concentration C versus time t"]
    nodeG["Compute C star minus C at each t"]
    nodeH["Plot ln of C star minus C versus t"]
    nodeI["Linear Regression to Obtain Slope"]
    nodeJ["KLa equals minus Slope in per second"]
    nodeK["Convert to per hour multiply by 3600"]
    nodeL["END Report Final KLa value"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```
