KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Define the isoelectric point (pI) of a protein. Why does a protein show minimum solubility at its isoelectric point?** **Model Answer:** The isoelectric point (pI) is the **pH at which a protein carries no net electrical charge**, i.e., the sum of positive and negative charges equals zero. At the pI, the protein molecules have **no electrostatic repulsion** between them. As a result: 1. Protein molecules approach each other closely. 2. **Van der Waals attractive forces** and **hydrophobic interactions** dominate. 3. Molecules aggregate and **precipitate out** of solution. 4. Consequently, **solubility is at a minimum**, and turbidity is at a maximum. **[3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for explanation of minimum solubility]** ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is a zwitterion? Show the structure of glycine at its isoelectric point.** **Model Answer:** A **zwitterion** is a molecule that contains **both a positive and a negative charge** but has a **net charge of zero**. The term is derived from the German *zwitter*, meaning "hybrid." At its isoelectric point (pH ≈ 5.97), glycine exists predominantly as a zwitterion: $$\text{H}_3\text{N}^{+}\text{–CH}_2\text{–COO}^{-}$$ Here, the amino group is **protonated** ($-\text{NH}_3^{+}$) and the carboxyl group is **deprotonated** ($-\text{COO}^{-}$), resulting in **zero net charge**. **[3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for structure]** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write the pI formula as $\text{pI} = \text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}$ (without the division by 2). **Deduct 1 mark** for this oversight. > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing $\text{p}K_a$ with $\text{pI}$ or with the $\text{pH}$ of the solution. Always clarify the quantity you are calculating. > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to state the **assumption that [Cation] = [Anion]** at the pI. **Deduct 1 mark** if not stated. > 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Not justifying **why solubility is minimum at pI** (students state it but do not explain *why*). > 5. **Common Mistake #5:** In titration problems, students often forget to **read $\text{p}K_a$ from the inflection point / buffering plateau**, not from the steepest slope (which is the equivalence point). ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(a)** Derive the relationship $\text{pI} = \dfrac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}$ for a neutral amino acid using the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation. State the key assumption clearly. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A protein sample shows the following absorbance (at 600 nm) when its pH is adjusted across a range. Determine its isoelectric point and explain the principle of the method used. **[7 marks]** | pH | Absorbance (600 nm) | |---|---| | 2.0 | 0.05 | | 3.0 | 0.18 | | 4.0 | 0.52 | | 5.0 | 0.81 | | 6.0 | 0.45 | | 7.0 | 0.20 | | 8.0 | 0.10 | | 9.0 | 0.06 | --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — Derivation:** **Step 1 — Write the two dissociation equilibria for a generic neutral amino acid:** $$\text{H}_3\text{N}^{+}\text{–CHR–COOH} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_3\text{N}^{+}\text{–CHR–COO}^{-} + \text{H}^{+} \quad (\text{p}K_{a1})$$ $$\text{H}_3\text{N}^{+}\text{–CHR–COO}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2\text{N–CHR–COO}^{-} + \text{H}^{+} \quad (\text{p}K_{a2})$$ **Step 2 — Apply Henderson-Hasselbalch to each:** $$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_{a1} + \log_{10} \left( \frac{[\text{Zwitterion}]}{[\text{Cation}]} \right)$$ $$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_{a2} + \log_{10} \left( \frac{[\text{Anion}]}{[\text{Zwitterion}]} \right)$$ **Step 3 — State the key assumption:** At the isoelectric point, **the concentration of the cationic species equals the concentration of the anionic species**, i.e., $[\text{Cation}] = [\text{Anion}]$, and the zwitterion is the dominant form. Therefore, the net charge on the molecule is zero. **Step 4 — Add the two Henderson-Hasselbalch equations:** $$2\,\text{pH} = \text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2} + \log_{10}\left( \frac{[\text{Zwitterion}]}{[\text{Cation}]} \right) + \log_{10}\left( \frac{[\text{Anion}]}{[\text{Zwitterion}]} \right)$$ $$2\,\text{pH} = \text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2} + \log_{10}\left( \frac{[\text{Anion}]}{[\text{Cation}]} \right)$$ **Step 5 — Substitute the pI condition** (where $\text{pH} = \text{pI}$ and $[\text{Anion}] = [\text{Cation}]$): $$2\,\text{pI} = \text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2} + \log_{10}(1)$$ Since $\log_{10}(1) = 0$: $$2\,\text{pI} = \text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}$$ **Step 6 — Final result:** $$\boxed{\text{pI} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}}$$ **[Marks Distribution: 2 for equilibria, 2 for Henderson-Hasselbalch application, 1 for assumption, 2 for algebraic derivation]** --- **Part (b) — Determination of pI from Turbidity Data:** **Step 1 — Identify the principle:** This method is based on **solubility behavior of proteins as a function of pH**. At the pI, the protein has zero net charge, exhibits minimum solubility, and hence **maximum aggregation/turbidity**. **Step 2 — Analyze the data:** The absorbance (turbidity) values are: 0.05, 0.18, 0.52, 0.81, 0.45, 0.20, 0.10, 0.06 **Step 3 — Identify the maximum:** The **maximum absorbance is 0.81**, which occurs at **pH = 5.0**. **Step 4 — Conclusion:** Since maximum turbidity (i.e., minimum solubility) occurs at pH = 5.0, the **isoelectric point (pI) of the protein is 5.0**. **Step 5 — Justification:** At pH 5.0, the protein carries no net charge. Without electrostatic repulsion, protein molecules aggregate via hydrophobic and van der Waals interactions, scatter light maximally, and are most amenable to precipitation. **[Marks Distribution: 2 for principle, 2 for data identification, 2 for pI value with reasoning, 1 for justification]** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the principle of isolation of proteins from aqueous systems by pH change. Describe a stepwise procedure to isolate casein from milk using this technique. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Explain how the isoelectric point of glycine can be determined experimentally by potentiometric titration. Sketch the expected titration curve and mark the key points. **[7 marks]** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — Protein Isolation by pH Change:** **Step 1 — Principle:** Proteins in aqueous solution carry a net charge that depends on the solution pH. At the **isoelectric point (pI)**, the net charge is zero. In this state: - Intermolecular **electrostatic repulsion** is eliminated. - **Hydrophobic interactions** dominate. - Proteins **aggregate and precipitate** out of solution. - This precipitate can be **recovered by centrifugation or filtration**. **Step 2 — Stepwise Procedure for Casein Isolation:** 1. **Defatting of milk:** Warm 100 mL of fresh skim milk to 40 °C. Optionally centrifuge at low speed to remove residual fat. 2. **Heating:** Heat the skim milk gently to **40–45 °C** to increase precipitation efficiency and reduce viscosity. 3. **pH adjustment:** Slowly add **1 M acetic acid dropwise** with continuous stirring until the pH reaches **4.6** (the pI of casein). Monitor using a calibrated pH meter. 4. **Floc formation:** A **white curd-like precipitate** of casein forms. Allow the mixture to stand for 10–15 minutes. 5. **Filtration/Centrifugation:** Filter through **muslin cloth** or centrifuge at 5,000 × g for 10 minutes. Collect the casein precipitate and discard the supernatant (whey). 6. **Washing:** Wash the precipitate twice with **cold distilled water** (pH 4.6) to remove adsorbed lactose, salts, and soluble proteins. 7. **Drying:** Dry the casein in a hot air oven at **50–60 °C** or under vacuum to obtain a powder. **Step 3 — Reaction (qualitative):** Casein (sodium caseinate, soluble at neutral pH) + Acid → Casein (insoluble curd, at pI = 4.6) + Sodium acetate (soluble). **[Marks Distribution: 3 for principle, 4 for procedure]** --- **Part (b) — Determination of pI of Glycine by Potentiometric Titration:** **Step 1 — Apparatus and Reagents:** - **0.1 M glycine** solution in 0.1 M $\text{HCl}$ (initially fully protonated, cationic form). - **0.1 M NaOH** titrant (carbonate-free). - **Calibrated pH meter** with glass electrode and reference electrode. - **Magnetic stirrer**, beaker, microburette. **Step 2 — Procedure:** 1. Pipette 50 mL of 0.1 M glycine in 0.1 M $\text{HCl}$ into a beaker. 2. Insert the pH electrode and start gentle stirring. 3. Add **0.1 M NaOH in 0.5 mL increments**, recording the pH after each addition. 4. Continue titration until **two equivalents** of NaOH have been added (i.e., past the second equivalence point). 5. Plot **pH (y-axis) vs. Volume of NaOH (x-axis)**. **Step 3 — Titration Curve Sketch (key features to mark):** - **Region 1 (0–1 equivalent):** First buffering region, centered at $\text{p}K_{a1} \approx 2.34$. - **First Equivalence Point:** ~1 equivalent NaOH added, pH ≈ 5–6, **steep rise**. - **Region 2 (1–2 equivalents):** Flat plateau region — this is the **zwitterion plateau**, centered at the **pI ≈ 5.97**. - **Second Equivalence Point:** ~2 equivalents NaOH added, pH ≈ 10, **steep rise**. - **Region 3 (>2 equivalents):** Second buffering region, centered at $\text{p}K_{a2} \approx 9.60$. **Step 4 — Calculation of pI:** $$\text{pI} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2} = \frac{2.34 + 9.60}{2} = 5.97$$ **Step 5 — Significance of curve regions:** - The **plateau (flat) regions** correspond to **buffering zones** where the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation is operative. - The **steep regions** correspond to **equivalence points**, where the predominant species changes abruptly (cation → zwitterion → anion). **[Marks Distribution: 2 for principle/setup, 2 for procedure, 2 for curve description with markings, 1 for calculation]** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know Points for KTU 2024 Exams:** - **Isoelectric Point (pI):** The pH at which a protein/amino acid has **zero net charge**. - **Zwitterion:** Molecule with both positive and negative charges but **net zero charge**. - **Master Formula (Neutral Amino Acid):** $\text{pI} = \dfrac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}$. - **For Acidic Amino Acids:** pI = average of the two lowest $\text{p}K_a$ values (carboxyl + side chain carboxyl). - **For Basic Amino Acids:** pI = average of the two highest $\text{p}K_a$ values (ammonium + side chain amine). - **Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation:** $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} \right)$. - **Solubility Behavior:** **Minimum at pI** (because no electrostatic repulsion between molecules). - **Turbidity Behavior:** **Maximum at pI** (because of maximum aggregation → maximum light scattering). - **Electrophoretic Mobility:** **Zero at pI**; direction reverses when pH crosses the pI. - **Isoelectric Precipitation:** Cheap, bulk, low-resolution; used as initial purification step. - **Critical Acid/Base:** Always add **slowly, dropwise, with continuous stirring**, monitor with calibrated pH meter (NOT pH paper). - **Titration Curve Reading:** $\text{p}K_a$ values are read from the **middle of the buffering plateaus**, NOT from equivalence points. - **Industrial Examples:** Casein (pI 4.6), soy protein (pI 4.5), whey proteins (pI 5.2). - **Order of Operations in Downstream Processing:** Clarification → **Isoelectric Precipitation** → Centrifugation → Chromatography (ion-exchange / affinity) → Polishing. - **Why proteins precipitate at pI:** Zero charge → no repulsion → hydrophobic and van der Waals forces dominate → aggregation → precipitation.

topics.title: Organic Salt Mediated Precipitation: Concentration of Proteins from Aqueous Systems by Addition of Ammonium Sulphate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBL508 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Organic Salt Mediated Precipitation: Concentration of Proteins from Aqueous Systems by Addition of Ammonium Sulphate ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Ammonium Sulphate [(NH₄)₂SO₄] Precipitation**, also called **"Salting-Out"**, is a classical, non-denaturing protein concentration and purification technique in which a high-ionic-strength neutral salt is progressively dissolved into an aqueous protein extract. As the salt concentration rises, the salt ions competitively strip the structured hydration shells (water of solvation) surrounding the dissolved protein molecules. This dehydrates the protein surface, reduces its net surface charge (charge-shielding), and collapses the protein's electrostatic interaction with water. The result is **aggregation, nucleation, and precipitation** of the protein out of solution while its native tertiary fold is largely preserved. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** *Ammonium sulphate precipitation is a fractional precipitation technique that exploits the inverse relationship between protein solubility and ionic strength at high salt concentrations, governed by the Debye–Hückel limiting law and the Cohn salting-out equation.* In the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course PCBBL508 – Bioprocess Engineering Lab), this experiment is classified as a **Module 2 – Downstream Processing (DSP) Stage 1 Recovery Operation**, immediately following cell disruption and preceding chromatography. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a crowded swimming pool where everyone (the protein molecules) is wearing a personal floatation belt (the hydration shell of water molecules). As a referee, ammonium sulphate ions pull the floatation belts away from swimmers one by one — first taking belts from the heaviest, least-soluble swimmers. Without their belts, those swimmers sink to the bottom (precipitate) first. By progressively throwing in **more salt**, the referee forces even the lighter, highly-soluble swimmers to lose their belts and sink too. You can therefore scoop swimmers off the bottom of the pool in stages, **separating the heavy from the light**. This sequential removal — first the most "salting-out sensitive" proteins at low salt, the rest at higher salt — is the essence of **fractional precipitation**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuitive Insight:** Salt does not *destroy* the protein — it simply removes its protective water coat. If the salt is later removed (by dialysis or desalting columns), the protein redissolves fully active. Hence ammonium sulphate is the **universal "first-concentration" tool** in any biochemistry lab. ### 1.3 Why Ammonium Sulphate Specifically? Out of dozens of possible salts (NaCl, KCl, MgSO₄, Na₂SO₄…), ammonium sulphate is preferred for the following textbook reasons: | Property | Value / Behaviour | |---|---| | **Solubility at 25 °C** | 4.1 M (~767 g/L) — extremely high | | **Ejecting (lyotropic) power** | Very high — near top of Hofmeister series | | **Effect on protein structure** | Stabilising; rarely causes denaturation | | **Heat of dissolution** | Mildly endothermic (does not heat the sample) | | **Cost** | Very inexpensive (~₹200/kg) | | **Density of saturated solution** | 1.235 g/mL — keeps protein particles suspended | | **Optical properties** | Transparent in UV — easy spectrophotometric assay | | **Interference with assays** | Low interference with Bradford/Lowry after desalting | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Trivia:** If asked *"Why not use NaCl?"*, the answer is — NaCl has high solubility but a much weaker salting-out power (lies lower in the Hofmeister series) and cannot reach sufficient ionic strength without saturating the solution first. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Domains of Salt–Protein Interaction The solubility (S) of a protein in salt solution is **biphasic** when plotted against ionic strength (I): **Phase 1 — Salting-IN (Low salt, I < 0.5 M):** At low ionic strength, the protein's ionisable side chains (–NH₃⁺, –COO⁻) repel each other because they carry net charges. Small quantities of salt ions shield these charges (Debye shielding), allowing the protein to dissolve *better*. Solubility therefore rises with ionic strength. **Phase 2 — Salting-OUT (High salt, I > 0.5 M):** At high ionic strength, salt ions strip the hydration shell. Hydrophobic patches are exposed, and protein–protein hydrophobic contacts dominate over protein–water contacts. Solubility drops logarithmically with ionic strength. > [!NOTE] > Ammonium sulphate precipitation exclusively uses the **salting-out** regime. ### 2.2 Core Theoretical Equations #### (a) The Cohn Salting-Out Equation (Backbone of the Experiment) The empirical relationship that defines the entire experiment: $$\log S = \log S_{0} - K_{s} \cdot I$$ Where: - **S** = Solubility of the protein (g/L) at the given ionic strength - **S₀** = Hypothetical solubility of the protein in pure water (extrapolated, g/L) - **Kₛ** = Salting-out constant (dimensionless, protein-specific; typical range 0.7 – 1.8) - **I** = Ionic strength of the salt solution (mol/L) **Interpretation:** A linear decrease in log-solubility with ionic strength. Proteins with a **larger Kₛ** precipitate first (at lower salt), proteins with a **smaller Kₛ** require more salt. #### (b) Ionic Strength (I) $$I = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_{i} \cdot z_{i}^{2}$$ For (NH₄)₂SO₄ which dissociates into 2 NH₄⁺ (z = +1) and 1 SO₄²⁻ (z = −2): $$I_{ammonium\,sulphate} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot [\,2c \cdot (1)^{2} + c \cdot (-2)^{2}\,] = \frac{1}{2} \cdot [2c + 4c] = 3c$$ So ionic strength = **3 × molarity** of ammonium sulphate. #### (c) Percentage Saturation (% S) $$\%\,S = \frac{\text{Mass of salt added}}{\text{Mass of salt required for full saturation in same volume}} \times 100$$ A **100% saturated (NH₄)₂SO₄ solution at 25 °C** contains **533 g of salt per 1 L of final solution** (this is the standard reference value used in all KTU problems). #### (d) Mass of Salt to Add — The KTU Favourite Equation To raise the saturation of a solution from an initial value **S₁ (%)** to a final value **S₂ (%)** in a given volume **V (L)** at **25 °C**: $$m_{(NH_{4})_{2}SO_{4}} = \frac{533 \cdot (S_{2} - S_{1})}{100 - 0.3 \cdot S_{2}} \cdot V \quad [\text{grams}]$$ The denominator term `100 − 0.3·S₂` is a **volume correction factor** — because ammonium sulphate powder displaces liquid volume. The coefficient 0.3 is empirically derived. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Variables & Units | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\log S = \log S_{0} - K_{s} \cdot I$ | S, S₀ in g/L; I in mol/L | Predict solubility at a given salt concentration | | 2 | $I = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum c_{i} z_{i}^{2}$ | c in mol/L; z = charge | Compute ionic strength of any salt | | 3 | $I_{(NH_{4})_{2}SO_{4}} = 3c$ | c = molarity of salt | Quick ionic strength lookup | | 4 | $m = \dfrac{533 \cdot (S_{2} - S_{1})}{100 - 0.3 \cdot S_{2}} \cdot V$ | m in grams; V in L; S in % | **Mass of ammonium sulphate to add** | | 5 | $\%\,S = \dfrac{m_{added}}{m_{saturated}} \times 100$ | m in g | Definition of % saturation | | 6 | $m_{saturated} = 533 \, \text{g/L at } 25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Mass per litre | Reference maximum | | 7 | $\text{Yield (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Total activity recovered}}{\text{Initial total activity}} \times 100$ | Activity in Units | Process efficiency | | 8 | $\text{Purification Fold} = \dfrac{\text{Specific activity after}}{\text{Specific activity before}}$ | U/mg protein | Purity assessment | | 9 | $\text{Specific activity} = \dfrac{\text{Enzyme activity (U)}}{\text{Total protein (mg)}}$ | U/mg | Diagnostic metric | | 10 | $\text{Recovery (\%)} = \dfrac{P_{after}}{P_{before}} \times 100$ | P = protein in mg | Protein mass balance | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** Almost every numerical problem in this module reduces to equation **(4)** above. Memorise it cold. ### 2.4 Real-World / Industrial Utility Ammonium sulphate precipitation is the **workhorse concentration step** in: - **Industrial enzyme manufacturing** (amylases, proteases from *Bacillus* / *Aspergillus* fermentation broths) - **Plasma fractionation** for human serum albumin, immunoglobulin, factor VIII - **Vaccine downstream processing** for antigen concentration - **Diagnostic kit manufacturing** (alkaline phosphatase, horseradish peroxidase bulk) - **Pharmaceutical R&D** — first capture step before ion-exchange or affinity chromatography In industrial columns, ammonium sulphate is being replaced by **tangential flow filtration (TFF)** for very large volumes, but in lab and pilot scales it remains dominant. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems ### 3.1 Derivation of the Cohn Salting-Out Equation (from First Principles) Starting from the **Debye–Hückel Limiting Law**, the activity coefficient (γ) of a dissolved protein in a salt solution is: $$\log \gamma_{\pm} = -A \cdot z^{2} \cdot \sqrt{I}$$ Where: - **A** = Debye–Hückel constant (≈ 0.509 in water at 25 °C for aqueous solutions) - **z** = effective net charge on the protein surface - **I** = ionic strength The solubility of the protein, by thermodynamic definition, depends on its activity coefficient. As salt ions accumulate, the protein's activity coefficient increases (γ grows as √I), and the effective chemical potential of the dissolved protein rises — driving it to leave the solution: $$\log \gamma_{protein} \propto z^{2}\sqrt{I}$$ Empirically, for proteins, this relationship collapses to a **linear dependence on I** (not √I) because the protein's "z²" appears to scale linearly with I due to counter-ion binding. Empirically: $$\log S = \log S_{0} - K_{s} \cdot I$$ Differentiating to find the slope: $$\frac{d(\log S)}{dI} = -K_{s}$$ Integrating from I = 0 to I = I: $$\int_{S_{0}}^{S} d(\log S) = -K_{s} \int_{0}^{I} dI$$ $$\log S - \log S_{0} = -K_{s} \cdot I$$ $$\boxed{\log S = \log S_{0} - K_{s} \cdot I}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the famous **Cohn equation**, after Edwin J. Cohn (Harvard, 1940s — plasma fractionation pioneer). ### 3.2 Derivation of the "533 g" Reference Mass The density of a saturated (NH₄)₂SO₄ solution at 25 °C is **1.235 g/mL**, containing approximately **4.06 M** of the salt. Converting to grams per litre: $$m = c \cdot M_{w} = 4.06 \, \text{mol/L} \times 132.14 \, \text{g/mol}$$ $$m = 536.5 \, \text{g/L}$$ The rounded, universally tabulated value is **533 g/L** (some sources cite 525–536 g/L depending on temperature; 25 °C is the KTU convention). ### 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Mass of Salt to Add (Direct Application) **Problem:** A 200 mL clarified bacterial extract is at **20% ammonium sulphate saturation**. You wish to bring it to **60% saturation** to precipitate your target enzyme. How many grams of crystalline (NH₄)₂SO₄ must you add? **Step 1 — Identify parameters** $$S_{1} = 20\%,\quad S_{2} = 60\%,\quad V = 200\,\text{mL} = 0.200\,\text{L}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the master formula** $$m = \frac{533 \cdot (S_{2} - S_{1})}{100 - 0.3 \cdot S_{2}} \cdot V$$ **Step 3 — Compute numerator** $$533 \cdot (60 - 20) = 533 \cdot 40 = 21320$$ **Step 4 — Compute denominator** $$100 - 0.3 \cdot 60 = 100 - 18 = 82$$ **Step 5 — Substitute and solve** $$m = \frac{21320}{82} \cdot 0.200$$ $$m = 260.0 \cdot 0.200$$ $$\boxed{m = 52.0\,\text{g of (NH}_{4})_{2}\text{SO}_{4}}$$ ### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Reverse Calculation (Finding % Saturation) **Problem:** You added **45 g of (NH₄)₂SO₄** to a **150 mL** extract initially at **0% saturation**. What is the final % saturation? **Step 1 — Rearrange the formula to solve for S₂** $$m \cdot (100 - 0.3 \cdot S_{2}) = 533 \cdot (S_{2} - S_{1}) \cdot V$$ $$100m - 0.3 m S_{2} = 533 V S_{2} - 533 V S_{1}$$ $$100m + 533 V S_{1} = S_{2} \cdot (533V + 0.3 m)$$ $$S_{2} = \frac{100m + 533 V S_{1}}{533V + 0.3m}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values** (m = 45 g, V = 0.150 L, S₁ = 0) $$S_{2} = \frac{100 \cdot 45 + 533 \cdot 0.150 \cdot 0}{533 \cdot 0.150 + 0.3 \cdot 45}$$ $$S_{2} = \frac{4500 + 0}{79.95 + 13.5}$$ $$S_{2} = \frac{4500}{93.45}$$ $$\boxed{S_{2} \approx 48.2\% \text{ saturation}}$$ ### 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Process Performance Calculation **Problem:** A crude extract contains **1200 mg total protein** and **850 U of enzyme activity**. After 60% ammonium sulphate precipitation and dialysis, the redissolved pellet contains **180 mg protein** and **720 U activity**. Calculate the **Yield (%)** and **Purification Fold**. **Step 1 — Initial specific activity** $$SA_{initial} = \frac{850\,\text{U}}{1200\,\text{mg}} = 0.708\,\text{U/mg}$$ **Step 2 — Final specific activity** $$SA_{final} = \frac{720\,\text{U}}{180\,\text{mg}} = 4.000\,\text{U/mg}$$ **Step 3 — Yield** $$\text{Yield} = \frac{720}{850} \times 100 = 84.7\%$$ **Step 4 — Purification Fold** $$\text{PF} = \frac{4.000}{0.708} = 5.65$$ $$\boxed{\text{Yield} = 84.7\%,\quad \text{Purification Fold} = 5.65\times}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A **2–10× purification fold** with **70–90% yield** is the textbook expectation for a well-executed ammonium sulphate cut. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mechanism Block Diagram — Why Salt Precipitates Protein ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Pure Aqueous Protein Extract] --> B[Add Solid NH4 2 SO4 Slowly] B --> C[Salt Dissolves into Ions] C --> D[NH4+ and SO4 2- Ions Compete for Water] D --> E[Hydration Shell Stripped from Protein] E --> F[Exposed Hydrophobic Patches on Protein] F --> G[Protein-Protein Hydrophobic Aggregation] G --> H[Visible Flocculent Precipitate] H --> I[Centrifugation / Filtration] I --> J[Pellet: Concentrated Target Protein] I --> K[Supernatant: Contaminants + Salt] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825 style E fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style H fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style J fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style K fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f ``` ### 4.2 Step-by-Step Laboratory Procedure Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Clarify cell extract by centrifugation 10000 rpm 20 min 4 C] --> S2[Step 2: Measure volume of supernatant and keep on ice] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Calculate mass of NH4 2 SO4 to reach target percent saturation] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Weigh calculated salt accurately on analytical balance] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Add salt in small aliquots over 30 to 60 minutes with gentle stirring at 4 C] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Equilibrate for 30 min to allow complete precipitation] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Centrifuge at 12000 rpm 20 min 4 C] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Decant supernatant carefully] S8 --> S9[Step 9: Dissolve pellet in minimal volume of assay buffer] S9 --> S10[Step 10: Assay protein concentration and enzyme activity] S10 --> S11[Step 11: Calculate yield percent and purification fold] S11 --> S12[Step 12: Store at minus 20 C or proceed to chromatography] style S1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0 style S5 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style S7 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315 style S11 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ### 4.3 Fractional Precipitation Strategy Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Crude Extract
Total Activity 1000 U] -->|Add salt to 30 percent S| B[Centrifuge] B -->|Discard pellet
Contaminants| C[Supernatant 1
850 U remaining] C -->|Add salt to 60 percent S| D[Centrifuge] D -->|Discard supernatant
Unwanted proteins| E[Pellet
Target enzyme
720 U] E -->|Resuspend in buffer| F[Partially Purified
Concentrate] style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1 style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style C fill:#e0f2f1,stroke:#004d40 style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style E fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ### 4.4 Hofmeister Series Reference (Lyotropic Power Ranking) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Strongly Hydrated Anions] --> B[SO4 2-] --> C[HPO4 2-] --> D[Cl-] --> E[Br-] --> F[NO3-] --> G[ClO4-] --> H[Strongly Salting Out] A2[Strongly Hydrated Cations] --> B2[NH4+] --> C2[K+] --> D2[Na+] --> E2[Li+] --> F2[Mg2+] --> G2[Al3+] style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style H fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c ``` > [!NOTE] > **Read the Hofmeister series from left to right:** anions on the LEFT (SO₄²⁻, HPO₄²⁻) are **strong salting-out** (dehydrate proteins); anions on the RIGHT (ClO₄⁻, SCN⁻) are **salting-IN** (increase protein solubility — destabilisers). --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** *Define "salting-out" of proteins. Why is ammonium sulphate the preferred salt for this purpose?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Salting-out is the reduction in protein solubility that occurs at high ionic strength, where salt ions strip the hydration shell surrounding the dissolved protein molecules, exposing hydrophobic patches and causing protein–protein aggregation and precipitation. Ammonium sulphate is preferred because (i) it has very high aqueous solubility (~533 g/L at 25 °C), (ii) it is a strong salting-out agent (high in the Hofmeister series), (iii) it has a stabilising effect on protein structure with minimal denaturation, (iv) it does not interfere strongly with common protein assays, and (v) it is inexpensive and readily available. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *What is meant by "percentage saturation" of ammonium sulphate? Mention its significance in fractional precipitation.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Percentage saturation of (NH₄)₂SO₄ is the ratio of the mass of salt dissolved in a given solution to the mass of salt required to make a fully saturated solution in the same volume, expressed as a percentage. A 100% saturated solution of (NH₄)₂SO₄ at 25 °C contains 533 g of salt per litre. Its significance lies in the fact that different proteins precipitate at different % saturation values — by performing the precipitation in stages (e.g., 0–30%, 30–60%, 60–80%), one can fractionate a complex mixture and obtain partial purification of the target protein. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > **Module 2 — Internal Choice Question Pattern** > Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. --- #### **Question A (14 marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain the principle of protein precipitation by ammonium sulphate. Discuss the Hofmeister series and justify why (NH₄)₂SO₄ lies at the top for salting-out applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the step-by-step laboratory protocol for concentrating an enzyme from a crude microbial extract using ammonium sulphate precipitation. What controls and parameters must be optimised? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Principle and Hofmeister Justification (7 marks):** - **Core principle** — At high ionic strength, salt ions (NH₄⁺ and SO₄²⁻) preferentially bind water molecules, withdrawing them from the protein hydration shell. This exposes hydrophobic patches on the protein surface, promoting protein–protein hydrophobic interactions and aggregation. Solubility decreases logarithmically with ionic strength (Cohn equation): $$\log S = \log S_{0} - K_{s} \cdot I$$ - **Biphasic behaviour** — Salting-in at low I (charge shielding) and salting-out at high I (dehydration) — the experiment operates exclusively in the latter regime. - **Hofmeister (lyotropic) series** — Anions ranked by their ability to salt-out proteins: $$\text{SO}_{4}^{2-} > \text{HPO}_{4}^{2-} > \text{Cl}^{-} > \text{Br}^{-} > \text{NO}_{3}^{-} > \text{ClO}_{4}^{-} > \text{SCN}^{-}$$ - **Why (NH₄)₂SO₄ is top of the list:** - The divalent SO₄²⁻ is the most strongly hydrated anion (highest charge density), so it strips water aggressively from proteins. - The combination of SO₄²⁻ (strongly hydrated anion) and NH₄⁺ (moderately hydrated cation) yields the highest molar salting-out strength without causing denaturation. - Solubility at 25 °C is 4.1 M — higher than Na₂SO₄ (1.9 M) or K₂HPO₄ — allowing access to a wider range of % saturations. - It preserves native structure due to kosmotropic (water-structuring) behaviour. **(b) Laboratory Protocol (7 marks):** 1. **Clarification** — Centrifuge the cell-free extract at 10,000 × g for 20 min at 4 °C to remove cell debris. 2. **Volume measurement** — Carefully measure supernatant volume and record temperature (should be 4 °C). 3. **Saturation calculation** — Decide target % saturation (typically chosen empirically by first running a pilot 0–20, 20–40, 40–60, 60–80, 80–100% cut). 4. **Salt addition** — Compute the required mass using: $$m = \frac{533 \cdot (S_{2} - S_{1})}{100 - 0.3 \cdot S_{2}} \cdot V$$ 5. **Slow addition with stirring** — Add solid salt in small portions over 30–60 min on a magnetic stirrer at 4 °C. Slow addition prevents local oversaturation. 6. **Equilibration** — Stir for an additional 30 min after the final addition to allow complete aggregation. 7. **Recovery** — Centrifuge at 12,000–15,000 × g for 20 min at 4 °C. Decant supernatant; drain pellet. 8. **Resuspension** — Dissolve pellet in minimal volume of suitable assay buffer (typically 1/10 to 1/20 of original volume). 9. **Desalting** — Dialyse, gel-filter (Sephadex G-25), or use ultrafiltration to remove residual salt before downstream assays or chromatography. 10. **Process control parameters:** - Temperature (4 °C universally to minimise proteolysis and denaturation) - pH (kept 1–2 units away from protein's pI to avoid isoelectric precipitation masking salt effect) - Rate of salt addition - Stirring speed (gentle — avoid foaming and shearing) - Equilibration time - Centrifugation speed and temperature --- #### **Question B (14 marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Derive the Cohn salting-out equation. Explain each term and how it is used to design a fractional precipitation strategy. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 250 mL clarified microbial extract containing 1500 mg of total protein and 900 U of enzyme activity is subjected to 70% ammonium sulphate saturation. After centrifugation and resuspension in 25 mL buffer, the pellet contains 220 mg protein and 720 U activity. Calculate: (i) the mass of (NH₄)₂SO₄ added (initially 0% saturation), (ii) the % yield of the enzyme, (iii) the purification fold. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Cohn Equation Derivation (7 marks):** - Start from the Debye–Hückel equation for activity coefficient: $$\log \gamma_{\pm} = -A z^{2} \sqrt{I}$$ - For proteins, empirical data show the dependence on I (not √I) due to multiple counter-ion binding sites scaling linearly with I. - The chemical potential μ = μ° + RT ln(γS). At equilibrium with solid protein, μ is constant, so a rise in γ drives S down. - Empirical relationship observed by Cohn: $$\log S = \log S_{0} - K_{s} \cdot I$$ - **Term meanings:** - **S**: solubility of the protein in salt solution (g/L) - **S₀**: hypothetical solubility in pure water (g/L, extrapolated) - **Kₛ**: salting-out constant, protein-specific; large Kₛ means the protein precipitates early - **I**: ionic strength of the salt (mol/L) - **Design utility:** Plot log S vs I for the target protein; the linear curve gives Kₛ. Then a fractional strategy is designed — perform successive cuts at I-values where contaminant Kₛ differs most from target Kₛ. **(b) Numerical Calculations (7 marks):** **(i) Mass of (NH₄)₂SO₄ added:** $$S_{1} = 0\%,\quad S_{2} = 70\%,\quad V = 0.250\,\text{L}$$ $$m = \frac{533 \cdot (70 - 0)}{100 - 0.3 \cdot 70} \cdot 0.250$$ $$m = \frac{533 \cdot 70}{100 - 21} \cdot 0.250$$ $$m = \frac{37310}{79} \cdot 0.250$$ $$m = 472.28 \cdot 0.250$$ $$\boxed{m \approx 118.07\,\text{g}}$$ **(ii) % Yield:** $$\text{Yield} = \frac{720}{900} \times 100 = 80.0\%$$ **(iii) Purification Fold:** $$\text{SA}_{initial} = \frac{900}{1500} = 0.600\,\text{U/mg}$$ $$\text{SA}_{final} = \frac{720}{220} = 3.273\,\text{U/mg}$$ $$\text{PF} = \frac{3.273}{0.600} \approx 5.45\times$$ $$\boxed{\text{PF} \approx 5.45\text{-fold}}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warnings > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Confusing % saturation with molarity.** A "70% saturated" solution is NOT 0.7 × 4.1 M = 2.87 M. It is defined by mass ratio to 533 g/L. > 2. **Forgetting the volume correction term `100 − 0.3·S₂`.** Skipping this introduces ~10–25% error at high % saturations. > 3. **Wrong units for V** — V must be in **litres**, not mL. Multiplying by mL gives an answer 1000× too large. > 4. **Mixing up purification fold and yield.** Yield = activity ratio; PF = specific-activity ratio. > 5. **Ignoring temperature** — The 533 g/L reference is for 25 °C. At 4 °C, the value drops slightly (~525 g/L). State the temperature assumed. > 6. **Resuspending pellet in original volume** — defeats the purpose of concentration. Use **minimal buffer** to achieve concentration factor. > 7. **Adding salt too fast** — produces local supersaturation, occludes contaminants, lowers purity. KTU lab marks include marks for technique. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Ammonium sulphate precipitation** is a **salting-out** technique operating in the high-ionic-strength regime. - ✅ Driven by **competition of salt ions for water of hydration** (kosmotropic effect). - ✅ Governed by the **Cohn equation**: $\log S = \log S_{0} - K_{s} \cdot I$. - ✅ For (NH₄)₂SO₄: ionic strength $I = 3 \times$ molarity (since 2 NH₄⁺ of charge +1, 1 SO₄²⁻ of charge −2). - ✅ **100% saturation = 533 g/L** at 25 °C — the universal reference value. - ✅ **Master mass formula**: $m = \dfrac{533 \cdot (S_{2} - S_{1})}{100 - 0.3 \cdot S_{2}} \cdot V$ — with V in litres. - ✅ **Hofmeister series ranking** — SO₄²⁻ at the top → strongest salting-out. - ✅ **Why (NH₄)₂SO₄?** High solubility + high salting-out power + non-denaturing + cheap. - ✅ **Procedure order**: Clarify → Measure V → Calculate m → Add slowly on ice → Equilibrate → Centrifuge → Resuspend pellet in minimal buffer → Desalt → Assay. - ✅ **Always perform at 4 °C** to prevent proteolysis and denaturation. - ✅ **Performance metrics**: Yield % (activity recovered) and Purification Fold (specific activity ratio). - ✅ **Typical expectation**: 2–10× purification fold, 70–90% activity yield in one well-designed cut. - ✅ **Salt must be removed** before ion-exchange chromatography (high salt competes for binding sites) and before most photometric assays. - ✅ **The technique is non-denaturing but not specific** — it concentrates *all* proteins in the chosen saturation window, so it is only a **partial purification / concentration** step, never a final polishing step.
